description: Unvalidated LLM finding outputs before evidence validation and filtering.

finding.summary: On reset (rst_ni deasserted or rst_6 asserted), the acct_wrapper initializes all acct_mem entries to 0xFFFFFFFF, granting full access to all peripherals from the moment reset is released, before any authorized configuration can be written.

evidence.description: Lines 81-85 show that when reset is active (~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)), all acct_mem entries are set to 32'hffffffff. This means every permission bit defaults to 1 (fully permissive), so all accesses are permitted until software explicitly restricts them.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes access control memory to all-ones (fully permissive), creating a window where all peripheral access is granted without authentication or privilege check.

evidence.description: Line 49: acc_ctrl_o is derived directly from acct_mem values. With acct_mem reset to 0xFFFFFFFF, acc_ctrl_o immediately drives all permission bits high after reset.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control output fed to all downstream peripherals is directly sourced from acct_mem, so the all-ones reset value propagates as full permission grants.

evidence.description: Lines 215-225: acc_ctrl drives acc_ctrl_c which is fanned out to all peripheral wrappers (AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, REGLK, ACCT). The acc_ctrl signal is driven by acc_ctrl_o from acct_wrapper, so the all-ones reset value immediately enables access to all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: The fully-permissive reset state of acct_mem propagates to all security-sensitive peripherals through acc_ctrl_c.

evidence.description: Line 517: rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]. Although this adds a privilege-level guard, its effectiveness depends on acc_ctrl_c being correctly restricted. After reset, acc_ctrl_c[*][0] is 1 for all privilege levels, so this guard is effectively bypassed during the post-reset window.
evidence.supports_claim: The privilege-level guard for bootrom access relies on acc_ctrl_c being properly configured; the all-ones reset default negates this protection until firmware reconfigures acct_mem.

evidence.description: Lines 1191-1195: AES0 wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][1], which is all-ones after reset, granting full AES0 access immediately at startup.
evidence.supports_claim: Downstream cryptographic peripheral (AES0) receives a fully-permissive access control signal due to the reset-to-all-ones behavior of acct_mem.

evidence.description: The acc_ctrl_o assignment at line 49 includes '|{8{we_flag}}' which ORs in we_flag bits unconditionally into the lowest permission word, meaning that if we_flag is asserted, access bits are forced to 1 regardless of what acct_mem contains, bypassing any software-configured restrictions.
evidence.supports_claim: The we_flag input can unconditionally override access control bits in acc_ctrl_o, bypassing the acct_mem-based permission configuration without any privilege or lock check.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module serves as the access control table for all security-sensitive peripherals in the design. Two concrete vulnerabilities exist: (1) On reset, acct_mem is initialized to all 0xFFFFFFFF, meaning all peripheral access is granted by default. This creates a window between reset release and software configuration where all peripherals are fully accessible to any privilege level. (2) The acc_ctrl_o output forcibly ORs in we_flag bits, allowing an external signal to unconditionally enable access bits regardless of the configured acct_mem values. Both issues mean that the access control mechanism can be bypassed, either during reset or via the we_flag override path.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged software executing immediately after reset, or any entity capable of asserting we_flag, can access cryptographic peripherals (AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC) and the register lock controller (REGLK) without proper authorization. This could enable key exfiltration, unauthorized cryptographic operations, or modification of the register lock state to further erode security boundaries.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The we_flag driving logic in riscv_peripherals is not fully shown in snippets (only the connection at line 1718 is visible). The exact conditions under which we_flag is asserted externally are not fully captured in the provided snippets. However, the structural violation of ORing we_flag into access control bits unconditionally is clearly visible in SNIP-0012 line 49.

finding.summary: On reset, the acct_wrapper initializes all acct_mem entries to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-ones), which grants maximum access permissions to all peripherals. This means that immediately after reset and before any privileged software programs the access control table, every privilege level has full access to all protected peripherals (AES, HMAC, SHA256, reglk, etc.).

evidence.description: On reset (~rst_ni || rst_6), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for every entry. acct_mem directly drives acc_ctrl_o, which is distributed to all peripheral wrappers as acct_ctrl_i. All-ones means all access-control bits are set, granting access rather than denying it at reset.
evidence.supports_claim: Unsafe reset behavior: access control memory initializes to all-ones (fully permissive) rather than all-zeros (fully restrictive), creating a window of unrestricted access after every reset.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is derived directly from acct_mem: assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}. When acct_mem holds 0xFFFFFFFF, acc_ctrl_o propagates all-ones to every downstream peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: The output access control bus carries all-ones after reset, distributing maximum permissions to all connected peripherals.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is formed as acc_ctrl_c[i][j] = acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i]). With acc_ctrl all-ones, acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][N] is always 1 for every privilege level and every peripheral index after reset.
evidence.supports_claim: All peripheral access enable signals are asserted for all privilege levels immediately after reset.

evidence.description: acct_ctrl_i (= acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][1]) is connected to aes0_wrapper. With acct_ctrl_i=1 after reset, the en gate (en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i) passes through and the peripheral is fully accessible regardless of privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: AES0 and all other peripherals are accessible to any privilege level after reset because acct_ctrl_i is 1.

evidence.description: Bootrom access uses: assign rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]. An adjacent commented-out line (//assign rom_req = rom_req_acct;) shows awareness of the guard but the active line also relies on acc_ctrl which is all-ones after reset.
evidence.supports_claim: Bootrom access control evaluates to fully open after reset, consistent with the all-ones initialization of acct_mem.

evidence.description: The reset clause sets acct_mem[j] <= 32'hffffffff for all j, the opposite of a secure default. A secure design would initialize to 0 (deny all) and require explicit privilege elevation.
evidence.supports_claim: Concrete violation: acct_mem reset value of 0xFFFFFFFF means all access control checks pass by default after reset, bypassing the intended privilege-based access control mechanism.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module stores per-peripheral access control policy in acct_mem. On every reset (synchronous or rst_6), acct_mem is initialized to 0xFFFFFFFF. This value is directly exported as acc_ctrl_o, reorganized into acc_ctrl_c indexed by privilege level, and fed as acct_ctrl_i to every protected peripheral (AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, reglk, bootrom). Each peripheral gates its enable signal with acct_ctrl_i, so with acct_ctrl_i=1 the gate is permanently open. This means that between reset and the point where privileged software re-programs acct_mem to restrictive values, all peripherals are accessible to all privilege levels. If software never programs the table, or if an attacker can trigger a reset, the protection is permanently absent.
finding.security_impact: All privilege-gated peripherals (AES key stores, HMAC, SHA-256, register lock controller, bootrom) are fully accessible to any privilege level after reset. An attacker who can trigger a reset, or any unprivileged code running before the secure OS initializes the table, can read or write cryptographic keys and configuration registers that should be restricted to machine mode or a trusted security domain.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show the exact timing of when the OS or firmware re-programs acct_mem after boot, so the duration of the vulnerability window is not precisely bounded. Additionally, only a subset of peripheral en assignments are shown (en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i for acct_wrapper); the exact en derivation for aes1, aes2, hmac, sha256, and reglk is not in the provided snippets, though the pattern is consistent across all modules shown.

finding.summary: AXI protection field (aw.prot and ar.prot) is hardwired to zero when driving the ACCT (Access Control) wrapper, stripping privilege/security/instruction-type information from all transactions before they reach the access-control decision logic.

evidence.description: Lines 1785 and 1796 unconditionally assign acct_axi_req.aw.prot and acct_axi_req.ar.prot to '0 (all-zero). AXI PROT[2:0] encodes {data/instruction, secure/non-secure, privileged/unprivileged}. Forcing these to zero means every transaction appears to the ACCT as a non-privileged, secure, data access, regardless of the actual CPU-side privilege level or security domain.
evidence.supports_claim: The aw.prot and ar.prot fields that carry privilege and security context are zeroed out before the request reaches the ACCT peripheral, preventing the access-control module from distinguishing privileged from unprivileged or secure from non-secure transactions.

evidence.description: The acct_axi_req signal (with the zeroed PROT fields) is fed directly into the i_acct_wrapper instance, which implements the platform access-control logic. The wrapper receives reglk_ctrl[6*8+7:6*8] as its register-lock input, showing it is intended to enforce privilege-based protection.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_axi_req with stripped PROT bits is the sole AXI request input to the ACCT wrapper; no privilege annotation reaches the access-control evaluation.

evidence.description: The acct_wrapper module exposes axi_req_i (receiving the zero-PROT request), reglk_ctrl_i for register-lock control, acct_ctrl_i for an additional access-control signal, and outputs acc_ctrl_o carrying access-control decisions for NB_PERIPHERALS peripherals. Correct operation depends on accurate privilege metadata in axi_req_i.
evidence.supports_claim: The ACCT wrapper produces acc_ctrl_o access-control values for all downstream peripherals; if it cannot see true privilege level, those decisions are based on incomplete security context.

finding.reasoning_summary: The AXI PROT field is the standard mechanism for conveying CPU privilege level and security domain to peripheral interconnects and access-control monitors. In riscv_peripherals.sv, lines 1785 and 1796 permanently tie aw.prot and ar.prot to zero before passing acct_axi_req to the ACCT wrapper. The ACCT wrapper (acct_wrapper.sv) is the platform's access-control enforcement point, producing acc_ctrl_o for all peripherals. Because it always sees PROT=0 (unprivileged, secure, data), it cannot differentiate a privileged supervisor/hypervisor access from a user-mode access, and it cannot differentiate secure from non-secure transactions. Any policy that relies on those bits is effectively bypassed.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged user-mode software agent can issue AXI transactions that the ACCT wrapper will evaluate as if they carry the same privilege level as a privileged (machine/supervisor) agent. This allows an attacker to bypass privilege-based access-control policies enforced by the ACCT module, potentially gaining unauthorized read or write access to protected peripherals across the platform.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal policy logic inside acct_wrapper (beyond the port declarations) is not shown in the provided snippets, so it is not confirmed whether the wrapper actually conditions decisions on axi_req_i.aw.prot/ar.prot. However, zeroing protection bits before an access-control block is architecturally incorrect and constitutes a security-relevant misconfiguration regardless of current internal usage, because future policy additions would also be silently bypassed.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper module resets acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-ones/permissive) on reset, and the acc_ctrl_o output unconditionally ORs the we_flag bitmask into the lowest access-control word, potentially bypassing lock protection. Additionally, the acct_wrapper receives only 8 bits of reglk_ctrl_i but internally uses a 16-bit reglk_ctrl, and the read/write guards use bit indices (e.g., reglk_ctrl[13]) that exceed the 8-bit input width, meaning those guard bits are always zero (unlocked).

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_i is declared as 8 bits wide ([7:0]) but is assigned directly to the internal 16-bit reglk_ctrl signal. Bits [15:8] of the internal reglk_ctrl will always be zero.
evidence.supports_claim: Guard bits reglk_ctrl[13] and reglk_ctrl[8..15] used in write checks are always zero because the input is only 8 bits, making those locks permanently open.

evidence.description: Write guards for acct_mem[03..05] use reglk_ctrl[13], and read guards use reglk_ctrl[12], but since reglk_ctrl is 16 bits and reglk_ctrl_i is only 8 bits, bits [13] and [12] are always 0, meaning these memory locations are never locked.
evidence.supports_claim: Bit indices beyond [7] in the 16-bit internal reglk_ctrl are always 0 because only the lower 8 bits are driven from input; lock checks at bit 13 (and 12 for reads) are permanently bypassed.

evidence.description: On reset (rst_ni=0 or rst_6=1), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for all entries. This means all access-control entries default to fully permissive (all bits set), rather than restrictive, creating an insecure default state.
evidence.supports_claim: Insecure reset value: acct_mem resets to all-ones (fully permissive access control), so a reset event causes all peripheral access controls to be maximally open.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o unconditionally ORs {8{we_flag}} into acct_mem[3*0+0], meaning when we_flag is asserted, the lowest access-control word is forced to all-ones regardless of the reglk_ctrl lock state. This bypasses the lock protection on acc_ctrl output.
evidence.supports_claim: we_flag can override access control output without any lock check, bypassing reglk_ctrl protection on acc_ctrl_o.

evidence.description: The acct_wrapper instantiation in riscv_peripherals passes reglk_ctrl[6*8+7:6*8] (8 bits) to reglk_ctrl_i, confirming the 8-bit input versus 16-bit internal usage mismatch.
evidence.supports_claim: Only 8 bits of lock control reach acct_wrapper, while internal logic uses 16-bit indices, leaving upper guard bits permanently zero.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1, meaning we_flag_1 can assert lock bits and prevent writes to aes0 registers even without the register lock being set—this is the inverse of the acct issue but shows inconsistent we_flag usage across wrappers.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent we_flag application: for aes0 it forces lock bits ON, while for acct it forces access-control output bits ON, demonstrating the design inconsistency in the permission chain.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper receives an 8-bit reglk_ctrl_i input but declares a 16-bit internal reglk_ctrl. The assignment 'assign reglk_ctrl = reglk_ctrl_i' zero-extends to 16 bits. Write guards for acct_mem[03..05] use reglk_ctrl[13] (always 0 = never locked), and read guards for those same entries use reglk_ctrl[12] (also always 0). This means those access-control memory regions can always be written and read regardless of the intended lock state. Additionally, on reset, acct_mem is set to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), which is the maximally permissive access-control value, creating an insecure default. The acc_ctrl_o output ORs we_flag unconditionally into the lowest word without checking reglk_ctrl, bypassing lock protection on that output path.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can trigger reset (rst_6) gains maximally permissive access-control state. The always-zero upper bits of reglk_ctrl mean that access-control table entries for certain peripheral groups (indices 3-5, covering acct_mem[03..05]) can never be locked by the register-lock mechanism, allowing any software to overwrite those access-control entries. The we_flag bypass on acc_ctrl_o can further override access control output independently of the lock.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full reglk_wrapper source showing how reglk_ctrl_o is populated is not in the snippets, so it is possible (but unlikely given the 8-bit port width shown in SNIP-0009 and SNIP-0018) that the upper bits are intentionally zero. The we_flag semantics in acct context versus aes0 context differ and the full design intent for we_flag is not fully visible.

finding.summary: In aes1_wrapper, key_reg1 readback is not protected by reglk_ctrl_i: key_reg0 and key_reg2 are zeroed when the lock bit is set, but key_reg1 is always returned in plaintext regardless of the lock state, allowing unauthorized readback of a secret AES key.

evidence.description: The read-data mux in aes1_wrapper gates key_reg0 reads behind reglk_ctrl_i[2] and key_reg2 reads behind a debug_mode_i zeroing, but key_reg1 has no corresponding guard: line 292 shows 'if (key_we0) rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[2] ? 32'b0 : key_reg0[...]' for key_reg0, while no analogous conditional is shown for key_reg1, meaning key_reg1 data can be read back without any lock check.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on key_reg1 readback path allows secret key material to be read even when the register-lock control bit is asserted.

evidence.description: key_reg0, key_reg1, and key_reg2 are declared as 256-bit AES key registers in aes1_wrapper. key_reg0 and key_reg2 receive debug_mode_i zeroing on the output path, but key_reg1 does not: 'assign core_key1 = {key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], ...}' with no debug_mode_i guard.
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 is a secret AES key register that lacks the same protection applied to key_reg0 and key_reg2.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_i is an 8-bit register lock control input to aes1_wrapper; individual bits are used to gate writes and reads to different register groups. Bit [2] is intended to protect key registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The lock control mechanism exists and is applied inconsistently across the three key registers.

evidence.description: Write-path lock checks use reglk_ctrl_i bits consistently: e.g., key_sel write is gated by reglk_ctrl_i[5], encdec_reg by reglk_ctrl_i[1], etc. However the read-path snippet shows key_reg1 is not included in the guarded readback case, unlike key_reg0.
evidence.supports_claim: The write path uses reglk_ctrl_i guards, making the absent read-path guard on key_reg1 an asymmetric and exploitable omission.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0004 shows core_key1 assigned directly from key_reg1 without a debug_mode_i guard (unlike core_key0 and core_key2). SNIP-0006 shows the read-data multiplexer gates key_reg0 readback with reglk_ctrl_i[2] but the corresponding guard for key_reg1 is absent. Together these constitute a sensitive readback vulnerability: an attacker can read secret key material stored in key_reg1 even when the register-lock bit intended to block such access is set.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access can exfiltrate the full 256-bit AES key stored in key_reg1 without triggering any register-lock protection, breaking the confidentiality guarantee of the AES peripheral and potentially compromising all data encrypted or decrypted with that key.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Only a partial read-path snippet is provided (SNIP-0006 ends at line 292); there may be additional case arms beyond what is shown that guard key_reg1 reads. The write-path snippet does not show a key_reg1-specific write guard either, which is consistent with the hypothesis but the full case statement is not visible. The aes0_wrapper and aes2_wrapper have analogous missing guards for key1/key2 that should also be reviewed.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper reset logic initializes acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF) on reset, which sets all access control entries to fully permissive values. When rst_6 is asserted (a module-specific reset separate from the system rst_ni), the acct_mem is reset to 0xFFFFFFFF, granting maximum access to all peripherals. This means any reset event on rst_6 restores unrestricted access control state, bypassing whatever restrictions were previously programmed.

evidence.description: On reset (when rst_ni is deasserted OR rst_6 is asserted), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for all entries. Since acct_mem directly controls peripheral access permissions (acc_ctrl_o is derived from acct_mem), resetting to all-ones sets all access control bits to 1, which represents fully permissive access. The condition `if(~(rst_ni && ~rst_6))` means asserting rst_6 alone is sufficient to trigger this permissive reset.
evidence.supports_claim: Asserting rst_6 resets acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), restoring fully permissive access control state and bypassing any previously programmed access restrictions.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is derived from acct_mem entries: `assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};`. This output propagates to riscv_peripherals.acc_ctrl and controls access to all peripherals. When acct_mem is all-ones after rst_6, acc_ctrl_o becomes all-ones, granting full access.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_mem values directly control peripheral access permissions via acc_ctrl_o, so resetting acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF grants unrestricted access to all protected peripherals.

evidence.description: rst_6 is declared as a separate reset signal in riscv_peripherals alongside other peripheral-specific resets (rst_1 through rst_13). It is connected to acct_wrapper's rst_6 input, enabling independent reset of the access control table without a full system reset.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 is a separate controllable reset signal that, when asserted, resets acct_mem to all-permissive values independent of the system reset rst_ni.

evidence.description: The acct_wrapper instantiation in riscv_peripherals connects rst_6 to the acct_wrapper, confirming the reset path is wired at the system integration level.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 is externally connected to acct_wrapper at the riscv_peripherals level, making it a valid attack vector if an attacker can assert this signal.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl is used to derive acc_ctrl_c for all NB_PERIPHERALS (14) peripherals. Resetting acct_mem to all-ones causes acc_ctrl_o to carry all-ones values, which propagates through acc_ctrl_c to grant access at all privilege levels to all 14 peripherals including AES, HMAC, SHA256, and REGLK.
evidence.supports_claim: All 14 peripherals lose their access restrictions when acct_mem is reset to all-ones, enabling any privilege level to access all security-critical peripherals.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module stores access control entries in acct_mem and resets them to 32'hffffffff (all-ones) when either rst_ni is deasserted or rst_6 is asserted (`if(~(rst_ni && ~rst_6))`). Since all-ones in acct_mem maps directly to all-permissive acc_ctrl_o output, asserting rst_6 at any time effectively grants unrestricted access to all 14 peripherals. This is a classic unsafe reset behavior where the reset state is maximally permissive rather than maximally restrictive. An attacker or untrusted software that can trigger rst_6 can bypass all access control restrictions that were previously programmed.
finding.security_impact: An attacker capable of asserting rst_6 can reset the entire access control table to all-permissive state (0xFFFFFFFF), bypassing all previously configured restrictions for all 14 peripherals including AES key stores, HMAC, SHA256, and register lock controller. This allows unauthorized access to cryptographic key material and sensitive registers across the entire security subsystem.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of rst_6 control (what generates or can assert it) is not shown in the snippets — it is only declared as a logic signal. If rst_6 is only assertable by a trusted reset controller and not by unprivileged software, the exploitability is reduced. However, the unsafe reset value (all-ones = all-permissive) is itself a design flaw regardless of rst_6 controllability.

finding.summary: Inconsistent debug_mode_i key zeroing: key2/key_big2 in aes0_wrapper, key_reg2 in aes1_wrapper, and ikey_hash in hmac_wrapper are not zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, while sibling key material (key0, key1, okey_hash) is zeroed. This allows sensitive cryptographic key material to leak through debug mode.

evidence.description: In aes0_wrapper, key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 (line 56) is assigned unconditionally without the debug_mode_i guard: `assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};`
evidence.supports_claim: key2 material is exposed during debug mode in aes0_wrapper while key0 and key1 are protected

evidence.description: In aes1_wrapper, core_key0 is zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted (line 149), but core_key1 (line 151) and by implication core_key2 lack the debug_mode_i guard, allowing key1 and key2 register contents to be exposed in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 and key_reg2 material is exposed during debug mode in aes1_wrapper while key_reg0 is protected

evidence.description: In aes2_wrapper, cii_K0 and cii_K1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted (lines 55-56), but cii_K2 (line 57) is also zeroed with debug_mode_i. However the pattern is inconsistent with aes0_wrapper where key2 is not guarded at all.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent application of debug_mode_i guard across wrapper instances creates unpredictable security posture

evidence.description: In hmac_wrapper, key (key0) is zeroed with debug_mode_i guard (line 54) and okey_hash is zeroed (line 56), but ikey_hash (line 55) is assigned unconditionally without debug_mode_i protection: `assign ikey_hash = {ikey_hash_bytes[0], ...};`
evidence.supports_claim: ikey_hash intermediate key material is exposed during debug mode in hmac_wrapper while key0 and okey_hash are protected

evidence.description: acct_wrapper resets acct_mem to 0xffffffff on reset (rst_ni && ~rst_6 de-asserted), initializing all access control bits to permissive/all-ones state, which could be an unsafe default depending on polarity convention.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes access control memory to all-ones, potentially granting all permissions by default

finding.reasoning_summary: Multiple cryptographic wrappers implement a debug_mode_i guard that zeros key material when debug mode is active, but the guard is applied inconsistently. In aes0_wrapper, key_big2 is not zeroed in debug mode while key_big0 and key_big1 are. In aes1_wrapper, core_key0 is zeroed but core_key1 and core_key2 lack the guard. In hmac_wrapper, ikey_hash is not zeroed in debug mode while key and okey_hash are. This inconsistency means sensitive cryptographic key material can be read back through debug interfaces even when debug protection is ostensibly enabled. Additionally, the acct_wrapper resets access control memory to all-ones (0xffffffff) which may represent a fully permissive state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or privileged debug user can extract partial or complete cryptographic key material (AES keys, HMAC keys) from the hardware security modules by exploiting the inconsistent debug_mode_i zeroing. The ikey_hash intermediate HMAC key and AES key2 material remain accessible in debug mode, potentially enabling cryptographic attacks or key recovery.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact semantics of debug_mode_i and whether it is accessible to untrusted parties is not shown in the snippets. The polarity of acct_mem all-ones initialization (whether 1=permit or 1=deny) is not confirmed from snippets alone. The full read-path logic is not shown, so it is possible (but unlikely given the assign statements) that additional guards exist on the output path.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper reset condition initializes access control memory (acct_mem) to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), granting maximum permissions on reset rather than denying all access. This means a reset event results in a fully permissive access control state for all peripherals.

evidence.description: On reset (~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for all entries. Since acc_ctrl_o is derived directly from acct_mem, this means all access control bits are set to 1 (permit) after reset, granting full access to all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes permission memory to all-ones, creating a fully permissive default access control state.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is computed as {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}} and is propagated to all peripheral wrappers as acct_ctrl_i. The access control output is directly derived from acct_mem, so the all-ones reset value translates to all-access-permitted.
evidence.supports_claim: The permission output sent to all peripherals is sourced from acct_mem which defaults to all-ones on reset.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is computed as acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i]), and is used as acct_ctrl_i for all peripheral wrappers. With acc_ctrl (sourced from acct_wrapper's acc_ctrl_o) set to all-ones after reset, acc_ctrl_c will be all-ones, granting access to every peripheral at every privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: All-ones acc_ctrl propagates to acc_ctrl_c, making acct_ctrl_i=1 for all peripherals after reset.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][1], which after reset is 1 (permit), enabling AES0 access for any privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: AES peripheral access is ungated after reset because the permission value defaults to all-ones.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper only performs register writes when (en && we), where en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i. With acct_ctrl_i=1 after reset, the access control gate is open for all AXI transactions, meaning key registers and plaintext can be written freely.
evidence.supports_claim: The acct_ctrl_i guard is ineffective after reset because it is initialized to 1 (permit-all).

evidence.description: Line 84: acct_mem[j] <= 32'hffffffff initializes the access control table to all-ones on reset. A secure system should initialize permission memory to deny-all (0x00000000) and require explicit privilege programming, not default to full access.
evidence.supports_claim: Fail-open reset of access control memory is a security vulnerability: any reset event grants all permissions to all peripherals.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper is the central access control table manager for the SoC peripheral fabric. On reset, it initializes its acct_mem to 32'hffffffff for all entries. This value propagates through acc_ctrl_o -> acc_ctrl -> acc_ctrl_c -> acct_ctrl_i to every peripheral (AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, reglk, acct). Because acct_ctrl_i=1 opens the 'en' gate in each peripheral wrapper, all peripheral registers become accessible to any privilege level immediately after reset. A secure design should default to deny-all (0) and require privileged software to explicitly grant access.
finding.security_impact: After any reset event, all cryptographic peripherals (AES0/1/2, SHA256, HMAC) and the register lock controller become fully accessible to any privilege level. An attacker with the ability to trigger a reset could exploit this window to read or write sensitive key material, plaintext, or modify register lock settings before privileged firmware reprograms the access control table.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is unknown how quickly after reset privileged firmware reprograms the acct_mem entries, which determines the size of the exploitable window. The chain does not include the aes1_wrapper, aes2_wrapper, hmac_wrapper, or sha256_wrapper reset/en logic snippets in full, but based on the consistent pattern seen in aes0_wrapper (en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i) and the all-ones reset default, the vulnerability is well-supported.

finding.summary: AXI aw.prot and ar.prot fields are permanently tied to zero for the AES0 wrapper, stripping all AXI protection attributes (privilege level, secure/non-secure, instruction/data) from every transaction reaching the AES cryptographic peripheral.

evidence.description: Lines 1250 and 1261 unconditionally assign aes0_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and aes0_axi_req.ar.prot = '0. These are the AXI4 AxPROT signals that encode privilege level (unprivileged=0/privileged=1), secure/non-secure access, and instruction/data type. Fixing them to zero means every write and read to the AES peripheral always appears as an unprivileged, secure, data access regardless of the actual requestor.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI protection bits that should reflect the initiator's privilege level are forced to zero, so any downstream protection logic that depends on AxPROT to enforce access control on the AES engine cannot distinguish privileged from unprivileged requestors.

evidence.description: The aes0_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i guards intended to enforce register lock and account-based access control, and debug_mode_i for debug gating. However, these guards receive their privilege-level input from priv_lvl_i via acc_ctrl_c, which is a separate side-channel; the AXI bus itself carries no privilege information because aw.prot/ar.prot are zeroed before reaching the wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: Even though per-privilege access control structures exist, the AXI path used by the wrapper carries zeroed protection bits, meaning any AXI-path protection enforcement inside the wrapper that checks AxPROT cannot function correctly.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper.axi_req_i is the sole AXI request interface into the AES cryptographic core, carrying all key and plaintext/ciphertext register accesses. With aw.prot and ar.prot permanently zeroed, the core cannot use AXI-layer privilege or security attributes to gate access.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES cryptographic peripheral is a high-value target; loss of AXI protection signaling on its sole bus interface weakens the layered security model.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0002 shows that both aw.prot and ar.prot of aes0_axi_req are hard-wired to zero by unconditional continuous assignments. The AXI4 specification uses AxPROT[0] to distinguish unprivileged from privileged access, AxPROT[1] for secure/non-secure, and AxPROT[2] for instruction/data. Zeroing these fields means the AES peripheral (SNIP-0003) always sees an unprivileged, secure, data-type transaction regardless of what entity initiated the bus cycle. While SNIP-0001 shows that a separate reglk_ctrl_i / acct_ctrl_i / debug_mode_i mechanism exists, these are side-channel inputs and do not compensate for the loss of AXI-native protection signaling. Any logic inside aes0_wrapper that gates access based on AxPROT will be permanently bypassed.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or non-secure bus master can access AES cryptographic registers (keys, plaintext, ciphertext, control) without any AXI-layer privilege check because AxPROT is always presented as zero. This could allow key extraction or injection by software running at a lower privilege level than intended, undermining the confidentiality and integrity guarantees of the hardware AES engine.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not confirmed whether aes0_wrapper internally re-checks AxPROT from axi_req_i or relies solely on the side-channel acct_ctrl_i / reglk_ctrl_i signals for all access control. If the internal wrapper entirely ignores AxPROT in favour of the side-channel signals, the impact of zeroing AxPROT is limited to any external AXI interconnect firewall that relies on the AxPROT attributes. The internal implementation of aes0_wrapper beyond its port declaration is not in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: key2 in aes0_wrapper and cii_K2 in aes2_wrapper are not zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, while key0 and key1 are zeroed. Additionally, the read-side logic in aes0_wrapper exposes key0 registers directly without a reglk_ctrl guard on key reads, creating a key readback vulnerability.

evidence.description: Lines 54-56 show that key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted ('assign key_big0 = debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 : ...'; 'assign key_big1 = debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 : ...'), but key_big2 is unconditionally assigned from key2 registers with NO debug_mode_i guard ('assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]}'). This means the third key slot is always exposed to the AES core even in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Incomplete debug-mode key zeroization: key2/key_big2 is leaked to the AES core during debug mode while key0 and key1 are correctly suppressed.

evidence.description: The read-side always block (lines 163-193) sets 'rdata = key0[address[8:3]]' as a default before the case statement (line 167). This default assignment exposes key0 register content for any address not covered by the case (addresses 5-10 map to key0 writes, but the read path default returns key0 array indexed by address). Key registers are readable without a reglk_ctrl_i[5] guard on the read default path, only specific addresses like ct_valid and ct have read locks applied.
evidence.supports_claim: AES key material (key0) is readable via the default branch of the read-side case statement without a reglk_ctrl guard blocking key readback.

evidence.description: In hmac_wrapper line 54, the HMAC key assignment correctly uses debug_mode_i to zero the key: 'assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[...]}'. However, line 55 shows 'ikey_hash' and subsequently 'okey_hash' (line 45) are assembled without debug_mode_i suppression, potentially leaking derived key material.
evidence.supports_claim: HMAC wrapper does not suppress ikey_hash or okey_hash during debug mode, only the raw key is zeroed.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i is an input port at line 22 of aes0_wrapper and is used in lines 54-56 to selectively zero key_big0 and key_big1 but not key_big2.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i signal exists as a protection mechanism but is inconsistently applied across the three key slots.

evidence.description: key0, key1, and key2 register arrays (lines 109-151) are the AES secret key material protected by reglk_ctrl_i[5] on writes. The read path (lines 163-193) lacks equivalent protection for key register reads via the default rdata assignment.
evidence.supports_claim: AES key registers are the sensitive resource that should be protected both on write (via reglk_ctrl) and on read (which is missing for key0 via the default path).

evidence.description: In aes2_wrapper lines 42, cii_K0, cii_K1, cii_K2 are declared but the source snippet for aes2_wrapper does not show debug_mode_i applied to any of the cii_K assignments, unlike aes0_wrapper which at least partially suppresses key0 and key1. This indicates aes2_wrapper may have no debug-mode key protection at all.
evidence.supports_claim: aes2_wrapper receives debug_mode_i but the snippet shows no conditional zeroization of key material comparable to aes0_wrapper, suggesting complete absence of debug-mode key suppression.

finding.reasoning_summary: The primary vulnerability is inconsistent application of the debug_mode_i protection signal. In aes0_wrapper, key_big0 and key_big1 are correctly zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 is not protected at all (line 56 of SNIP-0007). Since key_big is selected by key_sel and fed to the AES core, an attacker in debug mode who sets key_sel to select key_big2 can still use a real secret key. Secondary vulnerability: the read path in aes0_wrapper uses key0[address[8:3]] as the default rdata value (line 167 of SNIP-0008) before the case statement, meaning addresses not explicitly listed in the case will return raw key0 register content without any reglk_ctrl_i[5] read lock. The hmac_wrapper correctly zeros the key in debug mode but does not suppress derived intermediate hash values (okey_hash, ikey_hash). These represent concrete, source-evidenced security violations in permission/protection logic.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert debug_mode_i (or observe it being asserted) can: (1) force the AES engine to use key2 unmodified by selecting it via key_sel, bypassing the debug protection intended to prevent key exposure; (2) read back raw key0 register content through the unguarded default read path; (3) obtain HMAC intermediate hash values derived from the secret key. This breaks the confidentiality of AES and HMAC secret key material during debug sessions.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full aes2_wrapper source beyond the declaration section is not in the snippets, so the exact handling of cii_K0/K1/K2 under debug_mode_i cannot be fully confirmed from provided evidence alone. The hmac okey_hash exposure is inferred from the absence of debug_mode_i in its assignment, but the full hmac read path is not shown.

finding.summary: AES key material (key0, key1, key2) in aes0_wrapper is only protected against writes by reglk_ctrl_i[5], but key_big2 (derived from key2) is exposed unconditionally in debug mode: key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 is always output without any debug_mode_i guard, leaking key2 contents in debug mode.

evidence.description: Lines 54-56 show that key_big0 and key_big1 are conditionally zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted ('debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 : ...'), but key_big2 is assigned unconditionally as '{key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]}' with no debug_mode_i guard. This means key2 is always exposed even during debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: key_big2 lacks the debug_mode_i protection applied to key_big0 and key_big1, creating an inconsistent and incomplete protection of AES key material during debug mode.

evidence.description: Write-side register lock logic uses reglk_ctrl_i[5] to protect key0, key1, and key2 writes (lines 109-143). All three key arrays share the same reglk bit for write protection, but the debug readback suppression is inconsistently applied only to key0 and key1 (via key_big0/key_big1), not key2.
evidence.supports_claim: Write protection is consistent across all key arrays, but debug-mode readback protection is missing for key2/key_big2.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i fed from reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1 and debug_mode_i passed from the top-level debug_mode_i, confirming both signals are live inputs into the module.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is a real input from the system, making the missing guard on key_big2 exploitable whenever the system enters debug mode.

evidence.description: key0, key1, and key2 are AES cryptographic key registers written via the register interface and protected by reglk_ctrl_i[5] against unauthorized writes. Their contents are the sensitive assets at risk.
evidence.supports_claim: These are the AES key registers whose confidentiality is the security objective; key2 contents leak via key_big2 in debug mode.

finding.reasoning_summary: In aes0_wrapper, the three AES key arrays (key0, key1, key2) are all write-protected by reglk_ctrl_i[5]. For readback suppression during debug mode, key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is high, but key_big2 is assigned directly from key2 with no conditional zeroing. This is a clear inconsistency: an attacker or debug agent that asserts debug_mode_i can still read out the full key2 contents via key_big2, while key0 and key1 are protected. This represents a missing guard on a sensitive readback path.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to debug mode can extract the AES key2 value from aes0_wrapper even when the register lock (reglk_ctrl_i[5]) is set. This breaks the confidentiality of one third of the AES key material programmed into the wrapper, potentially allowing cryptographic operations to be decrypted or forged.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain does not include the full readback/output path of key_big2 to external ports, so it is not confirmed at the module boundary whether key_big2 is directly output or only used internally. However, the asymmetric treatment of key_big2 vs key_big0/key_big1 is directly visible in the source snippets and is sufficient to establish the finding.

finding.summary: AES key2 registers in aes0_wrapper lack debug_mode protection, while key0 and key1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted. This asymmetry means key2 material is exposed in debug mode.

evidence.description: Lines 54-56 show that key_big0 and key_big1 are forced to 192'b0 when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 is assigned unconditionally from key2 registers with no debug_mode_i guard: 'assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};'
evidence.supports_claim: key2 is not zeroed on debug_mode_i, unlike key0 and key1, creating an asymmetric protection gap.

evidence.description: Lines 140-151 show key2[0..5] are written under reglk_ctrl_i[5] lock, same bit as key0 and key1. The write-side lock is consistent, but the read/output path (key_big2) bypasses the debug_mode zeroing applied to key_big0 and key_big1.
evidence.supports_claim: key2 registers exist and are used in the same register-lock scheme, confirming they are sensitive key material that should be equally protected.

evidence.description: The assignment 'assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};' (line 56) is missing the 'debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 :' guard present on key_big0 and key_big1 (lines 54-55), directly exposing key2 material to the AES core and any downstream read path during debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing debug_mode guard on key2 output path allows key2 to be read out or used when debug mode is active, while key0 and key1 are correctly suppressed.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper instantiation in riscv_peripherals.sv passes debug_mode_i to the wrapper. The debug_mode_i signal is present and connected, confirming the intent to restrict key access in debug mode, making the missing guard on key2 a concrete omission.
evidence.supports_claim: The design explicitly intends to use debug_mode_i to suppress key material, but the suppression is incomplete for key2.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0014 lines 54-56 clearly show that key_big0 and key_big1 are conditionally zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 has no such conditional. This is a concrete asymmetry in the source code. SNIP-0015 confirms key2 holds sensitive AES key material protected by a register lock. SNIP-0004 confirms debug_mode_i is connected and intended as a protection signal. The missing guard on key_big2 means key2 is exposed whenever debug mode is active.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access (e.g., via JTAG) can read out or leverage key2 from the AES0 accelerator, potentially recovering up to 192 bits of key material from key2 while key0 and key1 are zeroed. This breaks the confidentiality of the AES key in the debug lifecycle state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The read-side path showing how key_big2 is ultimately exposed to an external interface is not fully shown in the snippets, but the absence of the debug_mode guard on key_big2 compared to key_big0/key_big1 is unambiguous in the source.

finding.summary: AES key material (key2) is exposed without debug_mode_i zeroing protection, while key0 and key1 are conditionally zeroed. Additionally, the read path exposes key register contents (key0) unconditionally before the register-lock case statement is evaluated.

evidence.description: Lines 54-56 show that key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 has no such protection: 'assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};' — debug_mode_i is not checked for key2.
evidence.supports_claim: key2 is always readable/usable regardless of debug mode, breaking the intended debug-mode key zeroing protection that exists for key0 and key1.

evidence.description: Line 167 shows 'rdata = key0[address[8:3]];' is assigned unconditionally before the case statement begins, meaning for any address not matched by the case (default path), the raw key0 value is returned in rdata without checking reglk_ctrl_i. The register-lock guard only applies within the explicit case entries.
evidence.supports_claim: AES key0 register contents can be read back via addresses not covered by the case statement, bypassing the register-lock check (reglk_ctrl_i[5]).

evidence.description: key_big0, key_big1, key_big2 are 192-bit AES key segments stored in registers key0, key1, key2 respectively, and are fed directly to the AES cipher core.
evidence.supports_claim: These are sensitive cryptographic key materials that must be protected from unauthorized readback or bypass.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_i[5] is used to guard writes to key0, key1, and key2 registers. The read path uses reglk_ctrl_i bits for read blocking. However, key2 read-back is not present in the case statement at all, meaning the default path returns key0 data for any unhandled address including key2 addresses.
evidence.supports_claim: The read-side register lock for key2 is absent from the case statement, and the default assignment exposes key0 data, creating an information leak.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i is used to zero key_big0 and key_big1 on lines 54-55, but line 56 omits this check for key_big2, creating an asymmetric and incomplete debug-mode protection.
evidence.supports_claim: Incomplete debug-mode zeroing allows key_big2 to be forwarded to the AES core even when debug mode is active, potentially enabling side-channel or direct key recovery in debug scenarios.

evidence.description: The aes0_wrapper is instantiated with debug_mode_i connected directly from the top-level riscv_peripherals, confirming the signal is live and the asymmetric zeroing has real security impact.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is a real system-level signal whose value controls key exposure; the missing zeroing of key2 is a concrete vulnerability.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities are present in aes0_wrapper. First, the debug_mode_i zeroing protection applied to key_big0 and key_big1 (lines 54-55) is absent for key_big2 (line 56), meaning the AES-192 key material from key2 registers is always forwarded to the cipher core regardless of debug mode. Second, in the read logic (line 167), rdata is pre-assigned to key0[address[8:3]] before the case statement, so any address not explicitly handled by the case falls through with raw key0 data, bypassing the reglk_ctrl_i register lock intended to block key readback. Together these represent missing guard and sensitive readback vulnerabilities.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access or with access to unguarded register addresses can recover AES-192 key material (key2 entirely, key0 partially via unguarded addresses). This breaks the confidentiality of cryptographic keys managed by the AES accelerator, potentially enabling decryption of protected data.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact set of addresses handled by the read case statement for key2 is not shown explicitly in the snippet (key2 read cases may be absent entirely), but the pre-assignment of rdata to key0 on line 167 and the missing debug_mode_i guard on key_big2 on line 56 are directly observable in the provided source snippets.

finding.summary: AES key register key2 (key_big2) is not zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, unlike key0 and key1, allowing debug mode to read back the AES-192 key material from key2.

evidence.description: Line 54 zeros key_big0 when debug_mode_i is asserted: 'assign key_big0 = debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 : ...'. Line 55 zeros key_big1 when debug_mode_i is asserted: 'assign key_big1 = debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 : ...'. However, line 56 assigns key_big2 WITHOUT any debug_mode_i guard: 'assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};'. This inconsistency means key2 key material is exposed through key_big2 even in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug mode protection applied to key_big0 and key_big1 is missing for key_big2, creating an asymmetric protection that leaks key2 to debug mode.

evidence.description: Line 199: 'assign key_big = key_sel[1] ? key_big2 : ( key_sel[0] ? key_big1 : key_big0 );' — when key_sel[1] is set, key_big2 (which lacks debug-mode zeroing) is selected as the active key fed directly to the AES cipher core at line 206.
evidence.supports_claim: key_big2 flows directly into the AES engine's key input, making the unguarded key2 material architecturally reachable and usable even in debug mode.

evidence.description: The read-side (lines 163-193) uses reglk_ctrl_i bits to gate rdata returns for various registers, but the key registers (key0/key1/key2) have no explicit read-lock case entries in the read mux — line 167 sets 'rdata = key0[address[8:3]]' as a default before the case statement, exposing key0 contents. Similarly key2 is not protected in readback. The write-side lock on key registers uses reglk_ctrl_i[5] to block writes but does not prevent readback of existing key values.
evidence.supports_claim: Incomplete read protection for key registers combined with missing debug_mode_i guard on key_big2 creates a path for key material exfiltration.

evidence.description: Line 1196 in riscv_peripherals.sv shows debug_mode_i is passed into aes0_wrapper. The intent is clearly to suppress key readout in debug mode, but the implementation is incomplete for key2.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i signal is explicitly wired to aes0_wrapper to enforce key protection, confirming the design intent that keys should be hidden in debug mode — making the omission on key_big2 a security defect.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippets show that key_big0 and key_big1 are guarded by debug_mode_i (forcing 192'b0 when in debug mode), but key_big2 at line 56 has no such guard. When key_sel[1] is asserted, key_big2 is routed as the active AES key. Additionally, the read path at line 167 sets rdata to key0 contents as a default before the case statement, and there are no case entries protecting key2 readback via the register interface. This creates two distinct leakage paths for key2 material: (1) it is not zeroed in debug mode and feeds the cipher core, and (2) the register readback interface does not block key register reads under lock conditions for key2.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access or register-read access can retrieve the AES-192 key2 material in plaintext. Since key2 is used as the active encryption key when key_sel[1]=1, this fully compromises the confidentiality of data encrypted with that key. This defeats the purpose of the reglk_ctrl and debug_mode_i protection mechanisms for one-third of the supported key set.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The read-side protection for key0/key1/key2 via the register interface is ambiguous — the case statement at lines 168-192 does not include entries for key register addresses (5-10, 20-31), meaning rdata falls through to the default assignment at line 167 which exposes key0. The same pattern likely applies to key1 and key2 but the snippet confirms the key2 debug_mode_i omission directly. It is unclear whether there is an external bus-level access control that would further restrict register reads.

finding.summary: AES key material (key0, key1) in aes0_wrapper is protected by reglk_ctrl_i[5], but key2 registers share the same lock bit. More critically, key_sel (a 2-bit register at line 36) is declared as an internal signal but no write case or lock guard for it appears in the register write block, meaning it is never written through the locked register path at all — leaving key selection unprotected. Additionally, key_big2 is assembled from key2 without the debug_mode_i zeroing guard that is applied to key_big0 and key_big1, allowing key2 material to be exposed even in debug mode.

evidence.description: key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted (lines 54-55), but key_big2 (line 56) is assembled from key2 with no debug_mode_i guard: `assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};`
evidence.supports_claim: key2 material is readable/usable in debug mode unlike key0 and key1, creating an inconsistent protection policy that exposes one of the three AES keys.

evidence.description: The register write block covers address cases 5-10 for key0 and 20-25 for key1 (guarded by reglk_ctrl_i[5]), and 26-31 for key2 (also guarded by reglk_ctrl_i[5]). However, there is no case statement entry for key_sel (declared as logic [1:0] at line 36), meaning key_sel is never written through any register write path and cannot be set by software — but its reset value is also not shown to be securely initialized in this block.
evidence.supports_claim: key_sel has no write path in the register interface, so its value at runtime depends entirely on reset/initialization, with no lock protection needed but also no way to configure it, which may be an incomplete implementation.

evidence.description: Line 56: `assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};` — unlike key_big0 and key_big1 which are conditionally zeroed when `debug_mode_i` is true, key_big2 is always driven from the stored key2 registers regardless of debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing debug_mode_i guard on key_big2 allows the key2 secret to leak in debug mode, bypassing the protection applied to the other two keys.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i is connected from riscv_peripherals to aes0_wrapper (line 1196), confirming this is an active signal that is supposed to suppress key output during debug.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug protection mechanism exists and is intentional, but is incompletely applied — only key0 and key1 are zeroed, not key2.

evidence.description: All key registers (key0, key1, key2) share the same reglk_ctrl_i[5] lock bit. There is no separate lock granularity for individual keys, and key_sel (the key selection signal) has no lock guard or write path in the case statement.
evidence.supports_claim: Coarse-grained locking means that locking or unlocking key registers affects all three keys simultaneously; key_sel protection is entirely absent.

finding.reasoning_summary: The aes0_wrapper contains three AES keys (key0, key1, key2) and a key_sel signal. The debug_mode_i input is intended to suppress secret key material by zeroing key_big0 and key_big1 when debug mode is active. However, key_big2 is assembled from key2 without any debug_mode_i conditional zeroing, meaning key2 is exposed in debug mode. This is a concrete missing guard visible directly in the source snippets. Additionally, key_sel has no write case in the register interface, making its value indeterminate beyond reset. The reglk_ctrl_i[5] lock bit covers all three keys with no per-key granularity.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access can read or influence AES key2 material through key_big2, since it lacks the debug_mode_i zeroing guard present for key0 and key1. This partial protection bypass could allow secret key extraction in debug scenarios, undermining the confidentiality of AES operations using key2.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-path logic for rdata is not shown in the provided snippets, so it is not confirmed whether key_big2 is directly readable via the register interface or only forwarded to the AES core. The key_sel initialization and usage path is also not fully visible. The security impact depends on whether key2 is used for sensitive operations and whether debug mode is accessible to untrusted parties.

finding.summary: AES key material (key2) is readable via rdata without any register-lock protection, and key2 internal signals bypass the debug_mode_i zeroing that protects key0 and key1.

evidence.description: key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted (lines 54-55), but key_big2 on line 56 has no debug_mode_i guard: 'assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};' — the key material in key2 is always exposed regardless of debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: key2 bypasses the debug_mode_i protection applied to key0 and key1, allowing key material to leak in debug mode.

evidence.description: The read-side memory map (lines 163-194) handles addresses 0-15 and a default case. Addresses 5-10 (key0), 20-25 (key1), and 26-31 (key2) have no read case entries in the address decoder — the default falls through to 'rdata = key0[address[8:3]]' (line 167), which directly exposes key0 array elements without checking reglk_ctrl_i[5]. Additionally, for addresses 26-31 (key2 range), there is no reglk_ctrl_i guard in the read path at all, meaning key2 registers are readable without any lock check.
evidence.supports_claim: Key material (key0 and key2) can be read back via rdata without the register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i[5]) being enforced on the read path.

evidence.description: The write path correctly guards key0/key1/key2 writes with reglk_ctrl_i[5] (lines 109-151), but the read path (lines 163-194) only guards addresses 0, 1-4, 11-15. Key register addresses 5-31 fall to the default 'rdata = key0[address[8:3]]' which has no lock check.
evidence.supports_claim: Asymmetric protection: writes to key registers are locked but reads are not, enabling key extraction.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i input is used to zero key_big0 and key_big1 (lines 54-55) but is completely omitted for key_big2 (line 56), creating an inconsistent debug protection policy across the three key slots.
evidence.supports_claim: Incomplete debug protection allows key2 content to flow to the AES core input even when debug_mode_i is asserted.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i as reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1 (line 1194), connecting the register lock controller output into the peripheral. The lock bits are available but the read path does not use them for key addresses.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock signal is present and delivered to the wrapper, confirming the missing read-side guard is a defect rather than an absent mechanism.

finding.reasoning_summary: The aes0_wrapper implements a register-lock mechanism (reglk_ctrl_i) that correctly guards writes to key registers. However, the read-side address decoder (always @(*) block, lines 163-194) only defines explicit cases for addresses 0-4 and 11-15. Key register addresses 5-31 fall through to the default assignment 'rdata = key0[address[8:3]]' which directly returns key0 array content without checking reglk_ctrl_i[5]. This means any bus master can read back AES key material even when the register lock is engaged. Additionally, key_big2 is assembled from key2 registers without the debug_mode_i zeroing that protects key_big0 and key_big1, creating an inconsistent debug isolation policy.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with bus access can read AES-192 key material (key0 and key2 registers) from aes0_wrapper even when the register-lock mechanism is set, bypassing the intended confidentiality protection. In debug mode, key2 content also flows unmasked into the AES core, potentially enabling side-channel or direct key recovery.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-path behavior for addresses in the key2 range (26-31) under the default case is inferred from the visible code pattern 'rdata = key0[address[8:3]]' — if address[8:3] exceeds key0 array bounds the simulator may return X, but the structural absence of reglk_ctrl_i[5] check on the read path is clear from the source. The we_flag_1 OR in the reglk connection (SNIP-0003) could override lock bits but its definition is not in scope.

finding.summary: Inconsistent debug_mode_i key zeroing in aes0_wrapper: key2 is not zeroed in debug mode unlike key0 and key1, allowing key material leakage. Similarly, in aes1_wrapper key_reg1 is not zeroed in debug mode while key_reg0 and key_reg2 are protected.

evidence.description: In aes0_wrapper, key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 (line 56) is assigned directly without any debug_mode_i guard: 'assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};' This means key2 material is exposed in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: key2 in aes0_wrapper is not protected by the debug_mode_i guard, unlike key0 and key1, resulting in selective key leakage during debug mode.

evidence.description: In aes1_wrapper, core_key0 and core_key2 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted (lines 149, 153), but core_key1 (line 151) is assigned directly from key_reg1 without any debug_mode_i guard: 'assign core_key1 = {key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], ...};' This inconsistency allows key_reg1 material to leak in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 in aes1_wrapper is not protected by the debug_mode_i guard, unlike key_reg0 and key_reg2, resulting in selective key leakage during debug mode.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper read path uses reglk_ctrl_i bits to gate register readback (e.g., key registers, results, control), showing the intended protection model for register lock. The missing debug guard on key1 is inconsistent with this protection intent.
evidence.supports_claim: The module implements register lock protections via reglk_ctrl_i, making the missing debug_mode_i guard on key_reg1 an anomalous inconsistency.

evidence.description: In aes2_wrapper, all three keys (cii_K0, cii_K1, cii_K2) are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted (lines 55-57), demonstrating the correct pattern that aes0_wrapper and aes1_wrapper fail to follow for their respective key2/key1 signals.
evidence.supports_claim: aes2_wrapper correctly zeros all key variants under debug_mode_i, confirming that aes0_wrapper and aes1_wrapper have incomplete debug protection.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippets show a pattern where debug_mode_i is used to zero out AES key material to prevent key exposure during debug mode. In aes0_wrapper, key_big0 and key_big1 are guarded but key_big2 is not. In aes1_wrapper, core_key0 and core_key2 are guarded but core_key1 is not. aes2_wrapper correctly applies the guard to all three keys. This inconsistency is a concrete source-code evidence of missing guards on sensitive key material.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can activate debug mode can read out AES key material (key2 in aes0_wrapper, key_reg1 in aes1_wrapper) that should be zeroed during debug. This could allow recovery of cryptographic keys used by the AES accelerators, breaking the confidentiality of encrypted data.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact mechanism by which debug_mode_i is asserted and the full read path for key_big2 in aes0_wrapper are not shown in the snippets, but the missing guard assignment is directly visible in the source.

finding.summary: The aes0_wrapper reset signal rst_1 is a separate software-controlled reset that, when asserted, clears sensitive AES key and state registers but does so without any privilege or register-lock (reglk_ctrl) check. An unprivileged or unauthorized software actor can assert rst_1 to unconditionally reset the AES engine state, potentially disrupting in-progress cryptographic operations or clearing keys in use.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals.sv declares a set of software-controlled reset signals (rst_1 through rst_5) alongside the hardware reset rst_ni. rst_1 is a separate logic signal that can be driven independently of the system reset and reglk protection.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_1 is a software-accessible reset path distinct from the hardware rst_ni, with no visible privilege or lock guard at the point of declaration.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with rst_ni and rst_1 connected directly (no gating by reglk_ctrl or privilege level for rst_1). The reglk_ctrl_i is OR'd with we_flag_1 for register write protection, but rst_1 is passed unguarded.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_1 reaches aes0_wrapper without any register-lock or privilege-level check at the instantiation boundary.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper declares AES key registers key0, key1, key2 (each 6x32-bit), plaintext p_c, and state registers as sensitive internal state. These are explicitly protected against writes via reglk_ctrl_i bits during normal operation.
evidence.supports_claim: The key and state registers are the protected resources that can be cleared via rst_1.

evidence.description: The write-side always block uses reglk_ctrl_i bits (e.g., reglk_ctrl_i[5] for keys, reglk_ctrl_i[3] for p_c, reglk_ctrl_i[7] for state) to prevent unauthorized writes during normal operation. However, the reset condition (rst_ni OR rst_1) is evaluated before this check and unconditionally clears registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl guard only covers the 'else if(en && we)' path; the reset path via rst_1 bypasses all reglk_ctrl checks.

evidence.description: At lines 95-96, the always block resets key and state registers when (~rst_ni || rst_1). The rst_1 condition is ORed with ~rst_ni, meaning asserting rst_1 alone (without deasserting the hardware reset) is sufficient to clear all protected AES key and state registers, regardless of the reglk_ctrl_i lock state.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_1 provides an unguarded path to clear AES key material and state, bypassing all reglk_ctrl register-lock protections.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i is used to zero out key_big0 and key_big1 outputs (lines 54-55), showing the design has explicit debug-mode key suppression. However, the rst_1 reset path is not similarly gated by debug_mode_i or any privilege check.
evidence.supports_claim: The design has partial debug protections for key readback but not for the rst_1-triggered key clear, indicating an inconsistency in the security model.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper's reglk_ctrl_i is protected with we_flag_1 OR gating, but rst_1 is passed directly without any equivalent protection, meaning any bus master that can write to the RST_CTRL peripheral can force a reset of the AES0 engine and clear its key material.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthorized clearing of AES key registers via rst_1 can cause denial-of-service on cryptographic operations or be used to force re-keying in a manner observable by an attacker.

finding.reasoning_summary: The aes0_wrapper has two reset inputs: rst_ni (hardware system reset) and rst_1 (software-controlled reset from the RST_CTRL peripheral). The write-protection logic using reglk_ctrl_i bits guards against unauthorized register writes during normal operation, but the reset path triggered by rst_1 bypasses all of these guards. When rst_1 is asserted, the sensitive AES key registers (key0, key1, key2) and state/plaintext registers are unconditionally cleared. At the instantiation site in riscv_peripherals.sv, rst_1 is connected without any privilege-level check or reglk_ctrl gating, unlike the reglk_ctrl_i connection which is OR'd with a we_flag. This means any software with access to the RST_CTRL peripheral can clear AES key material regardless of the register lock state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the RST_CTRL register interface can assert rst_1 to unconditionally clear AES encryption keys and state in aes0_wrapper, bypassing all reglk_ctrl register-lock protections. This enables: (1) denial-of-service on in-progress AES operations, (2) forced clearing of provisioned keys, and (3) potential oracle attacks by resetting state at chosen moments during encryption.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippet for the actual reset always block beginning (showing the full condition with rst_1) is partially visible in SNIP-0015 at lines 95-96 ('else if(en && we)'), but the reset condition itself is referenced in the graph edges (stmt reads rst_1 and rst_ni) without the explicit 'if(~rst_ni || rst_1)' line being in the provided snippets. The RST_CTRL register interface that drives rst_1 is not fully shown, so the exact privilege level required to assert rst_1 is uncertain. The we_flag_1 OR gating on reglk_ctrl_i for aes0 (SNIP-0004 line 1194) is present but not for aes1/aes2, which may indicate intentional asymmetry.

finding.summary: Inconsistent debug_mode_i key zeroing: key2 in aes0_wrapper and key_reg2 in aes1_wrapper are not zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, while key0/key1 equivalents are zeroed. Similarly, ikey_hash is not zeroed in hmac_wrapper under debug_mode_i.

evidence.description: In aes0_wrapper, key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 is assigned directly from key2 registers without any debug_mode_i guard: `assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};`
evidence.supports_claim: key2 in aes0_wrapper leaks its value even in debug mode because the debug_mode_i zeroing logic is missing for that key slot.

evidence.description: In aes1_wrapper, core_key0 is zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted (`assign core_key0 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : ...`), but the corresponding assignment for core_key1 at line 151 shows no debug_mode_i guard: `assign core_key1 = {key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], ...}`. key_reg2 likewise has no shown zeroing guard.
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 and key_reg2 in aes1_wrapper are not zeroed under debug_mode_i, exposing sensitive key material during debug sessions.

evidence.description: In aes2_wrapper, cii_K0 and cii_K1 are zeroed under debug_mode_i, but cii_K2 is also zeroed here (`assign cii_K2 = debug_mode_i ? 128'b0 : ...`). This is consistent within aes2. However the pattern of inconsistency across modules is still present.
evidence.supports_claim: aes2_wrapper correctly zeros all three keys, confirming the design intent is to zero keys in debug mode - making the omission in aes0_wrapper (key2) and aes1_wrapper (key1, key2) a clear vulnerability.

evidence.description: In hmac_wrapper, `ikey_hash` is assigned directly from ikey_hash_bytes without any debug_mode_i guard (`assign ikey_hash = {ikey_hash_bytes[0], ...}`), while `key` and `okey_hash` are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted. This exposes the inner HMAC key hash in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: ikey_hash leaks sensitive HMAC inner key data during debug mode because the debug_mode_i protection is applied inconsistently - omitted for ikey_hash but present for key and okey_hash.

evidence.description: key0, key1, key2 in aes0_wrapper are AES cryptographic key registers whose confidentiality must be protected. The inconsistent application of debug_mode_i zeroing means key2 is readable/usable in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: AES key material is the sensitive resource that should be protected from exposure in debug mode.

finding.reasoning_summary: The design intent is clear: when debug_mode_i is asserted, cryptographic key material should be zeroed before being passed to the AES/HMAC cores. This is correctly done for some keys (key0, key1 in aes0_wrapper; key0 in aes1_wrapper; all keys in aes2_wrapper; key and okey_hash in hmac_wrapper) but inconsistently omitted for others (key2 in aes0_wrapper; key1 and key2 in aes1_wrapper; ikey_hash in hmac_wrapper). This creates a security vulnerability where an attacker with debug mode access can extract partial key material from the unguarded slots.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert debug_mode_i (e.g., through JTAG or a debug interface) can read or cause the use of real key material from the unprotected key slots (key2 in aes0, key1/key2 in aes1, ikey_hash in hmac), potentially enabling cryptographic key recovery. This defeats the purpose of the debug mode key-zeroing protection.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-back path for the key registers is not shown in the snippets - it is possible additional read-side guards exist. Also, the exact mechanism by which debug_mode_i is asserted is not shown, so the exploitability depends on the threat model for debug access.

finding.summary: AES key material (key2) is not protected by debug_mode_i zeroing, unlike key0 and key1. In aes0_wrapper, key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 (derived from key2) is unconditionally exposed. Additionally, key2 registers can be written through wdata when reglk_ctrl_i[5] is not set, and there is no debug mode write-protection either.

evidence.description: Lines 54-56 show that key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted ('debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 : ...'), but key_big2 is always assigned from key2 without any debug_mode_i guard: 'assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};'
evidence.supports_claim: key2 is not protected by the debug_mode_i zeroing logic that protects key0 and key1, creating an inconsistent protection that allows key2 to be read back in debug mode.

evidence.description: Lines 141-145 show key2 registers (key2[5] through key2[3]) are written when reglk_ctrl_i[5] is not set and en&&we is true. The same reglk bit [5] guards key0 and key1, but there is no additional debug mode write-block for key2.
evidence.supports_claim: key2 can be written via wdata whenever the register lock bit is clear, and unlike key0/key1, its readback path (key_big2) is never suppressed in debug mode.

evidence.description: The asymmetry between the debug_mode_i guard on key_big0/key_big1 (forced to 192'b0) versus key_big2 (no guard) means that when the CPU enters debug mode, an attacker with debug access can read key2 content through key_big2 while key0 and key1 are suppressed.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent debug-mode protection leaves key2 as a sensitive readback path not covered by the same zeroing defense applied to key0 and key1.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i is passed directly from riscv_peripherals to aes0_wrapper at line 1196, confirming that the debug mode signal reaches the module and is used to conditionally zero key_big0 and key_big1 but not key_big2.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i signal is architecturally connected and used as a protection gate, making the missing guard on key_big2 a concrete, exploitable gap.

finding.reasoning_summary: The aes0_wrapper module implements a debug-mode zeroing guard for key_big0 and key_big1 (both derived from key0 and key1 respectively), but omits the same guard for key_big2 (derived from key2).
