When debug_mode_i is asserted, key0 and key1 derived outputs are forced to zero, preventing sensitive key material from leaking via debug interfaces. However, key2's derived output key_big2 is always the actual key value regardless of debug mode. Since key2 is a full 192-bit AES key component and key_big2 feeds directly into the AES core, this selective protection creates a vulnerability where key2 material can be observed in debug mode.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access (e.g., via JTAG or a debug exploit) can read AES key2 material through the key_big2 signal path while key0 and key1 are correctly suppressed. This breaks the confidentiality of AES key material and could allow full recovery of the cryptographic key if key2 is used as part of the encryption key.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact readback path for key_big2 to a bus-accessible register is not shown in the provided snippets; however, the missing debug_mode_i guard on key_big2 compared to key_big0 and key_big1 is directly visible in the source. The security impact depends on whether key_big2 feeds an observable output, which is architecturally likely given the symmetric treatment of key_big0 and key_big1.

finding.summary: The acct_ctrl_i (access control) signal is received by all peripheral wrappers (aes0, aes1, aes2, sha256, hmac, acct, reglk) but is never used to gate or guard register reads or writes within those modules. The en_acct signal is declared alongside en but the actual register access logic uses only en, leaving acct_ctrl_i/en_acct without functional enforcement.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is constructed from acc_ctrl indexed by priv_lvl_i and then distributed as acct_ctrl_i to all peripherals. This is the intended privilege-level access control signal.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control signal is privilege-level-aware at the distribution point.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i connected from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][2], alongside reglk_ctrl_i. Both are passed as separate inputs to the module.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_ctrl_i is wired into aes1_wrapper but its enforcement inside the module must be verified.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper declares acct_ctrl_i as an input (line 57) and also declares both en and en_acct as internal signals (line 140). The en_acct signal is declared but the actual write logic (SNIP-0018) and read logic (SNIP-0019) gate on 'en' and 'reglk_ctrl_i' only.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_ctrl_i and en_acct are declared but there is no evidence they gate register access in aes1_wrapper.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper declares 'en' and 'en_acct' as separate logic signals (line 140), but all register write and read logic uses only 'en' (e.g., 'if (en && we)' in SNIP-0018, 'if (~en)' in SNIP-0019). en_acct is never shown guarding any access path, meaning acct_ctrl_i has no functional effect on reads or writes.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control signal is declared but unused as a guard, allowing any privilege level to read/write AES registers if en is asserted.

evidence.description: Register write logic in aes1_wrapper checks 'en && we' and reglk_ctrl_i bits, but does not check acct_ctrl_i or en_acct. Any bus master can write AES key, block, and control registers regardless of privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing acct_ctrl_i guard on write path means privilege-based access control is bypassed.

evidence.description: Register read logic in aes1_wrapper gates on 'en' and reglk_ctrl_i bits only. Sensitive registers including core_result (encrypted/decrypted data) and key registers are readable by any privilege level since acct_ctrl_i/en_acct are not checked.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing acct_ctrl_i guard on read path enables sensitive data readback without privilege enforcement.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper read/write logic (lines 94-144) gates memory operations on 'en' and reglk_ctrl bits only. The acct_ctrl_i input and en_acct signal are declared (SNIP-0012 line 27, 44) but are not used to guard reads or writes of acct_mem, which holds peripheral access-control values.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control table (acct_mem) itself is unprotected by the acct_ctrl_i signal, allowing unprivileged modification of the entire access control policy.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper holds acct_mem which provides acc_ctrl_o, the source of all peripheral access control values. Writes to acct_mem are guarded only by reglk_ctrl bits, not by acct_ctrl_i.
evidence.supports_claim: The root access control table is the critical protected resource whose integrity depends on correct permission enforcement.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper declares acct_ctrl_i (line 29) and en_acct (line 43) but the register lock output (reglk_ctrl_o) and the reglk_mem read/write logic do not reference acct_ctrl_i, meaning unprivileged software can modify register lock values.
evidence.supports_claim: Register lock configuration is not guarded by access control, undermining the entire reglk protection mechanism.

finding.reasoning_summary: Across all peripheral wrappers (aes0, aes1, aes2, sha256, hmac, acct, reglk), the acct_ctrl_i signal is declared as a port and en_acct is declared as an internal signal, but neither is used in the actual register read/write guard logic. The write paths check only 'en && we' with optional reglk_ctrl_i bit guards, and read paths check only 'en'. The en_acct signal that should implement the access control enable is dead code. This means the privilege-level-based access control system (which computes acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][peripheral_index]) has no functional effect on peripheral register access. Most critically, the acct_wrapper (which stores the access control table) and reglk_wrapper (which stores register lock values) are themselves unguarded by acct_ctrl_i, allowing any software to modify the security policy.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged software agent can read sensitive registers (AES keys, HMAC keys, encrypted results) and write to all peripheral control registers, AES key registers, and cryptographic control registers. Worse, it can overwrite the access control table (acct_mem) and register lock values (reglk_mem), completely disabling the hardware security policy. This constitutes a full bypass of the privilege-based peripheral access control system.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The AXI-to-internal signal bridge (the logic that assigns en and en_acct from axi_req_i) is not shown in the snippets. It is possible en_acct is used there instead of en for gating the AXI transaction acceptance, but even if so, the internal register logic uniformly uses 'en' not 'en_acct', so the access control is at best applied at the AXI level only and not at the register level, which would still be a weaker-than-intended protection.

finding.summary: AXI protection bits (aw.prot and ar.prot) are hardwired to zero for all AES1 AXI requests, bypassing any privilege-level or secure/non-secure transaction signaling that the AXI protocol provides.

evidence.description: Lines 1336 and 1347 explicitly assign aes1_axi_req.aw.prot and aes1_axi_req.ar.prot to '0 (all zeros). AXI PROT[1] encodes the privileged/unprivileged access level and PROT[1] the secure/non-secure attribute. Forcing both to zero means every request to the AES1 peripheral is always treated as unprivileged, non-secure, and data access regardless of the actual privilege level of the initiating transaction.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI protection signaling is statically suppressed, so the AES1 peripheral cannot distinguish privileged from unprivileged accesses based on AXI PROT bits.

evidence.description: The aes1_wrapper instantiation does accept reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs intended for access control, but the AXI request fed to it already has its PROT bits zeroed before reaching the wrapper, so any internal AXI-PROT-based gating would always see unprivileged/non-secure.
evidence.supports_claim: Even though per-register lock and account-control signals are present, the AXI privilege signaling path is disabled by the forced-zero PROT assignment upstream.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper.axi_req_i is the sole AXI request input to the AES1 cryptographic peripheral, which holds sensitive key material and operates on plaintext/ciphertext. Incorrect PROT values affect whether any AXI-PROT-aware protection inside the peripheral can enforce privilege boundaries.
evidence.supports_claim: The resource being accessed (AES1 cryptographic engine) is security-sensitive and relies on correct AXI signaling for privilege enforcement.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0002 shows that both aw.prot and ar.prot are permanently tied to '0 for all transactions routed to the AES1 wrapper. The AXI4 PROT field carries privilege-level (bit 0), secure/non-secure (bit 1), and instruction/data (bit 2) attributes. Zeroing these unconditionally means every AES1 access appears as an unprivileged, non-secure, data transaction irrespective of the actual initiator privilege level. SNIP-0001 shows the wrapper does receive separate reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i signals, but those are independent of the AXI PROT path. If the AES1 wrapper or any downstream monitor relies on AXI PROT bits to enforce access restrictions, that enforcement is permanently defeated.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged software entity could issue AXI transactions to the AES1 peripheral and the PROT bits would be indistinguishable from a privileged, secure-world transaction. Any AXI-PROT-based firewall or internal check that should deny unprivileged access to AES key registers or ciphertext would be bypassed, potentially exposing secret key material or allowing unauthorized cryptographic operations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal implementation of aes1_wrapper is not fully shown; it is unclear whether the wrapper itself enforces privilege checks based on AXI PROT bits or relies solely on reglk_ctrl_i/acct_ctrl_i. If the wrapper ignores PROT entirely and only uses the out-of-band control signals, the practical impact of zeroing PROT may be limited. Additionally, whether an upstream AXI firewall already filters requests before this point is not visible in the provided context.

finding.summary: The AES1 block_reg (plaintext input register) can be read back directly via the register interface without any register-lock protection guarding the read path for block addresses, and the write path uses reglk_ctrl_i[5] which is a shared lock bit also covering key_sel, creating a coarse-grained protection scheme. More critically, SNIP-0019 shows that key registers are readable (e.g., key_reg0 is returned on reads when key_we0 is asserted), and the block_reg read path is not shown to be gated by any reglk bit, enabling sensitive plaintext readback.

evidence.description: The read-side logic in aes1_wrapper returns key_reg0 contents directly when key_we0 is asserted (line 292: 'if (key_we0) rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[2] ? 32'b0 : key_reg0[address[5:3]]'). There is no corresponding guard shown for block_reg reads — the case statement covers ADDR_RESULT* and status but does not show a protected case for ADDR_BLOCK*, meaning block_reg contents fall through to the default which returns 32'b0, yet the key registers are explicitly readable under a single reglk bit check. This means an attacker who can manipulate reglk_ctrl_i[2] to 0 can read back AES key material directly from the register interface.
evidence.supports_claim: Key register contents (key_reg0) are exposed via the read path gated only by reglk_ctrl_i[2], a single lock bit that can be bypassed if the lock register is not set.

evidence.description: block_reg write is protected by reglk_ctrl_i[5] (line 259). The same bit also guards key_sel writes (line 252). This shared lock bit means that unlocking key_sel also unlocks block_reg writes, creating a coarse protection boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: block_reg shares its write-lock bit with key_sel, so disabling the lock for one operation disables it for both sensitive resources.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i connected to reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8] from riscv_peripherals, without any 'we_flag' OR-masking that is present for aes0 (SNIP-0004 shows 'reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1' for aes0 but aes1 has no such additional hardening).
evidence.supports_claim: AES1's register lock input lacks the we_flag hardening present in AES0, making its lock state solely dependent on the reglk_ctrl register value which can be written if the reglk wrapper's own lock is not set.

evidence.description: AES0 instantiation applies 'reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1' as reglk_ctrl_i, adding an extra hardening layer. AES1 (SNIP-0005) does not apply an equivalent we_flag, showing an inconsistency in protection.
evidence.supports_claim: AES1 is missing the we_flag OR-hardening applied to AES0, leaving its lock state without the additional enforcement present in a peer module.

evidence.description: block_reg is declared as a 128-bit plaintext input register (4x32-bit array) used to feed the AES core. It holds the plaintext to be encrypted, making it a sensitive resource requiring strong read and write protection.
evidence.supports_claim: block_reg holds AES plaintext input, making its readback a sensitive information disclosure risk.

finding.reasoning_summary: The aes1_wrapper exposes key_reg0 as readable via a case-statement branch guarded only by reglk_ctrl_i[2]. The block_reg write path is protected by reglk_ctrl_i[5] shared with key_sel, creating coarse-grained protection. AES1 lacks the we_flag OR-hardening present in AES0's instantiation in riscv_peripherals. Together, these issues mean that if an attacker can write to the reglk register (when its own lock bit is unset at reset or by a privileged operation), they can clear the lock bits and subsequently read back AES key material through the register interface, and write arbitrary values to the block and key registers.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the AXI bus who can clear the reglk_ctrl register (possible before locks are set, or via a privileged soft-reset) can read AES key material from key_reg0 via the aes1_wrapper register interface, and can write arbitrary plaintext/key values without restriction, completely compromising the confidentiality of data processed by the AES1 engine.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-side case statement for block_reg addresses is not shown in the snippets (only key and result read paths are visible). The exact conditions under which reglk_ctrl can be modified at runtime are not fully traced. The we_flag mechanism for AES0 is referenced but its source and security guarantee are not fully elaborated in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: In aes1_wrapper, block_we is derived purely from address range comparison without checking reglk_ctrl_i, and the block register write path uses reglk_ctrl_i[5] as the lock bit — but the key write enables (key_we0/1/2) also use no reglk check in the enable signal itself. More critically, the block_we signal is a pure combinational decode of the address with no privilege or lock guard in its definition, and key_reg1 reads back without any reglk protection.

evidence.description: block_we is assigned purely based on address range: `assign block_we = ((address[11:3] >= ADDR_BLOCK0) && (address[11:3] <= ADDR_BLOCK3));` with no reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i qualification. The lock check only occurs later at the register update stage.
evidence.supports_claim: block_we itself carries no permission guard; it is a pure address decode signal.

evidence.description: The block register write uses reglk_ctrl_i[5]: `block_reg[address[4:3]] <= reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? block_reg[address[4:3]] : wdata;`. However, key_reg1 write enable key_we1 is also derived without a lock check in its definition, and at line 258-259 the block_we is checked but the key_we paths (key_we0/1/2) use a separate reglk_ctrl_i[3] or similar — but key_we1 writes to key_reg1 which is NOT zeroed by debug_mode_i (unlike key_reg0 and key_reg2).
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 is not cleared when debug_mode_i is asserted, unlike key_reg0 and key_reg2.

evidence.description: At lines 149-153: `core_key0 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {...key_reg0...}` and `core_key2 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {...key_reg2...}` but `core_key1 = {key_reg1[7],...key_reg1[0]}` with NO debug_mode_i zeroing. This means key_reg1 is always forwarded to the AES core regardless of debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 content is passed to the AES core even in debug mode, leaking key material when debug_mode_i is asserted.

evidence.description: Key register readback at line 292: `if (key_we0) rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[2] ? 32'b0 : key_reg0[address[5:3]];` — only key_reg0 readback is shown with reglk protection. The snippet shows key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback paths are not visible here, raising concern about completeness of read-side protection.
evidence.supports_claim: Read-side protection for key_reg1 and key_reg2 may be missing or incomplete.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper instantiation passes reglk_ctrl_i from reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8] without any additional OR with a we_flag (unlike aes0_wrapper which ORs with we_flag_1). This means aes1's lock bits have no hardware write-once override path.
evidence.supports_claim: aes1_wrapper lacks the we_flag override present in aes0_wrapper, a potential inconsistency in protection policy.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper receives `reglk_ctrl_i ( reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1 )` providing an additional hardware lock-override, while aes1_wrapper at SNIP-0005 receives `reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8]` with no we_flag OR, showing inconsistent protection across AES instances.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent application of the we_flag protection mechanism across crypto peripheral instances.

finding.reasoning_summary: The primary vulnerability is that core_key1 (derived from key_reg1) is forwarded to the AES core without being zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, unlike core_key0 and core_key2 which are explicitly zeroed. This means an attacker with debug mode access can still recover key material stored in key_reg1 by observing AES operations. Additionally, block_we is a pure address decode with no lock guard in its own logic, relying entirely on a downstream conditional in the register update — this is a design fragility. Secondary issues include the missing we_flag protection for aes1 compared to aes0.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access (debug_mode_i=1) can still load and use key_reg1 content through the AES core, leaking up to 256 bits of key material. This defeats the intended debug isolation for the AES1 key registers.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The read-side snippet (SNIP-0018) is truncated and may not show full key_reg1/key_reg2 readback protection. The we_flag_1 logic definition is not shown, so the full impact of the aes0 vs aes1 asymmetry is partially inferred from the instantiation differences.

finding.summary: The AES1 wrapper block register (block_reg) feeding core_block is protected by reglk_ctrl_i[5] on writes, but core_block is assembled and passed directly to the AES core without any debug_mode_i zeroing guard, unlike key_reg0 and key_reg2 which are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted.

evidence.description: core_key0 and core_key2 are explicitly zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted: 'assign core_key0 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {...key_reg0...}'. This establishes the pattern that sensitive registers should be suppressed in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Key registers have debug_mode_i protection; block register does not.

evidence.description: core_block is assigned directly as '{block_reg[3], block_reg[2], block_reg[1], block_reg[0]}' at line 159 with no debug_mode_i guard. Unlike core_key0/core_key2, there is no conditional zeroing when debug_mode_i is asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: core_block lacks the debug_mode_i suppression present for key registers, allowing the plaintext block to be read/used by the AES core during debug mode.

evidence.description: Write protection for block_reg uses reglk_ctrl_i[5]: 'block_reg[address[4:3]] <= reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? block_reg[...] : wdata'. Read protection for result registers uses reglk_ctrl_i[4]. There is no debug_mode_i check for block_reg reads or the core_block signal path.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock and privilege controls exist but no debug_mode_i suppression is applied to core_block.

evidence.description: The read path for key registers at lines 292-294 applies reglk_ctrl_i[2] protection but there is no read path entry for block_reg, and core_block is fed directly to the AES core without suppression. Result registers (ADDR_RESULT0-3) are guarded by reglk_ctrl_i[4].
evidence.supports_claim: The block data can flow into the AES core and potentially be observable via results even in debug mode, because core_block is not suppressed.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i connected to reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8] (no we_flag OR'd in, unlike aes0_wrapper which ORs we_flag_1), acct_ctrl_i, and debug_mode_i. The missing we_flag OR for aes1 may also weaken lock enforcement compared to aes0.
evidence.supports_claim: aes1_wrapper receives debug_mode_i and reglk_ctrl but the wrapper does not apply debug_mode_i to core_block, unlike the key registers.

finding.reasoning_summary: The AES1 wrapper applies debug_mode_i zeroing to core_key0 and core_key2 (the AES key inputs), preventing key material from reaching the AES core during debug mode. However, core_block, which carries the plaintext input to the AES core, is assembled directly from block_reg with no equivalent debug_mode_i suppression. This asymmetry means that during debug mode an attacker with debug access could observe the AES core operating on real plaintext even though key material is partially suppressed. Additionally, aes1_wrapper's reglk_ctrl_i lacks the we_flag OR seen in aes0_wrapper, potentially weakening lock enforcement for aes1.
finding.security_impact: During debug mode, core_block (AES plaintext input) is not zeroed, allowing debug-mode access to observe or trigger AES operations on real plaintext data. Combined with results potentially readable via ADDR_RESULT registers (protected only by reglk_ctrl_i[4], not debug_mode_i), an attacker with debug access could recover plaintext/ciphertext relationships or manipulate AES block operations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full aes1_core module instantiation and its handling of core_block in debug mode is not shown. It is possible the AES core itself suppresses outputs in debug mode, but no such evidence is present in the provided snippets. The reglk_ctrl we_flag omission for aes1 vs aes0 may be intentional or a bug but cannot be fully confirmed without the we_flag_2 definition.

finding.summary: The AES1 wrapper's core_init signal is derived from init_reg, which can be written when reglk_ctrl_i[1] is deasserted. However, key registers protected by reglk_ctrl_i[3] and block registers protected by reglk_ctrl_i[5] use different lock bits than the init/next/encdec controls (reglk_ctrl_i[1]). More critically, the read path allows key material readback (key_reg0/1/2) when reglk_ctrl_i[2] is 0, and result readback when reglk_ctrl_i[4] is 0, with these being independently controllable lock bits. The AES1 wrapper instantiation in riscv_peripherals.sv omits the 'we_flag' OR-masking that is applied to AES0's reglk_ctrl, meaning AES1 receives only the raw reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8] without any additional write-enable flag protection that AES0 enjoys.

evidence.description: AES0 wrapper instantiation shows reglk_ctrl_i is passed as 'reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1', which ORs in an additional write-enable flag to strengthen the lock.
evidence.supports_claim: AES0 has an additional 'we_flag_1' protection layered on top of reglk_ctrl, which AES1 lacks.

evidence.description: AES1 wrapper instantiation passes 'reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8]' directly without any OR-masking with a we_flag signal, unlike AES0 which uses 'reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1'. This means AES1's register lock protection is weaker than AES0's.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing we_flag OR-mask for AES1 means AES1 register locks can be bypassed in scenarios where we_flag would have provided additional protection.

evidence.description: Write-side register protection in aes1_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl_i[1] to gate init_reg, next_reg, and encdec_reg writes; reglk_ctrl_i[3] for key length registers; reglk_ctrl_i[5] for block and key select registers. These are distinct bits, so partial lock configurations are possible.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock scheme uses separate bits per register group, so a misconfigured reglk_ctrl could leave sensitive registers (e.g., key registers) writable while others are locked.

evidence.description: Read-side: key_reg0/1/2 are returned when reglk_ctrl_i[2]==0; AES result registers are returned when reglk_ctrl_i[4]==0; control/status registers when reglk_ctrl_i[0]==0. If reglk_ctrl is not properly set, key material and AES results are readable.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key material and AES ciphertext/plaintext results are readable through rdata when corresponding reglk_ctrl bits are not set.

evidence.description: core_init is assigned as 'init_reg & ~init_reg_d', derived directly from init_reg which is writable when reglk_ctrl_i[1]==0. No additional privilege or access check beyond reglk_ctrl_i[1] and the en/we signals.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES core initialization signal can be triggered by writing to ADDR_CTRL when reglk_ctrl_i[1] is deasserted, with no further privilege check in the write path.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_i is an 8-bit input to aes1_wrapper, providing per-bit lock control for different register groups within the AES1 peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: The lock control signal is the primary access enforcement mechanism for all AES1 registers.

finding.reasoning_summary: The AES1 wrapper instantiation in riscv_peripherals.sv passes reglk_ctrl bits to AES1 without the additional 'we_flag' OR-masking applied to AES0. This creates an asymmetric security posture where AES1 has weaker lock protection than AES0. Within AES1 itself, the lock bits are applied per register group using different reglk_ctrl_i bit positions. If any of these bits are not asserted, the corresponding registers (including key material readable via reglk_ctrl_i[2], and AES results via reglk_ctrl_i[4]) are accessible. The missing we_flag for AES1 means an attacker who can control reglk_ctrl to omit the we_flag contribution could access AES1 resources that would be locked in AES0.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can influence the reglk_ctrl values (e.g., through privilege escalation or misconfiguration) could read back AES key material or AES encryption results from the AES1 peripheral due to weaker lock enforcement compared to AES0. Additionally, they could trigger AES core initialization or write to key/block registers to influence cryptographic operations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact definition and source of 'we_flag_1' used for AES0 is not present in the snippets, so the full scope of what protection is missing for AES1 cannot be fully quantified. The reglk_ctrl initialization and update logic is not shown, so it is unclear whether the default state of reglk_ctrl leaves registers locked or unlocked. The acct_ctrl_i access control check at line 201 provides some additional gating, but its interaction with reglk_ctrl is not fully analyzed here.

finding.summary: In aes1_wrapper, core_key1 is assembled from key_reg1 without any debug_mode_i zeroing guard, unlike core_key0 and core_key2 which are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted. This asymmetry allows the AES key slot 1 material to leak to the AES core during debug mode.

evidence.description: core_key0 (line 149) and core_key2 (line 153) are conditionally zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted: 'assign core_key0 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {...}' and 'assign core_key2 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {...}'. However, core_key1 (line 151) has no such guard: 'assign core_key1 = {key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], ...}' — the full key register contents are always exposed.
evidence.supports_claim: Key slot 1 (key_reg1) is not protected against readback/use in debug mode, while key slots 0 and 2 are.

evidence.description: Line 151: 'assign core_key1 = {key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], key_reg1[5], key_reg1[4], key_reg1[3], key_reg1[2], key_reg1[1], key_reg1[0]};' — missing debug_mode_i guard. When debug_mode_i is high, core_key1 still carries the real key material, unlike core_key0 and core_key2 which are forced to zero.
evidence.supports_claim: The missing debug_mode_i zero-guard on core_key1 is the concrete violation.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i is declared as an input port of aes1_wrapper at line 58, confirming the module is designed to receive and act on a debug mode signal.
evidence.supports_claim: The module has an explicit debug_mode_i input intended to suppress key exposure in debug mode, making the missing guard on core_key1 a clear design error.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates aes1_wrapper and connects the system-level debug_mode_i signal to it (line 1282). The reglk_ctrl for aes1 is connected without the 'we_flag' OR that aes0 has (compare SNIP-0003 line 1194 vs SNIP-0004 line 1280), but more critically debug_mode_i reaches the wrapper where the asymmetric key zeroing exists.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i signal path from the top-level into aes1_wrapper is confirmed, making the missing guard exploitable.

evidence.description: In aes0_wrapper, both key_big0 (line 54) and key_big1 (line 55) are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, showing the intended security pattern: 'assign key_big0 = debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 : {...}'. aes1_wrapper follows this for key0 and key2 but not key1.
evidence.supports_claim: The intended pattern across modules is to zero all key outputs in debug mode; aes1_wrapper violates this for key slot 1.

evidence.description: The read path (lines 292-294) allows key_reg1 to be read back directly via the bus interface: 'if (key_we1) rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[2] ? 32'b0 : key_reg1[address[5:3]]'. Combined with core_key1 being passed to the AES core unguarded, key_reg1 material is accessible both at the bus read side and through the cryptographic core during debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Both direct register readback and core key forwarding paths expose key_reg1 in debug mode.

finding.reasoning_summary: aes1_wrapper intentionally zeroes core_key0 and core_key2 when debug_mode_i is asserted to prevent key leakage in debug mode. However, core_key1 (line 151) lacks the same guard and unconditionally exposes key_reg1. This is inconsistent with the security intent demonstrated by the debug_mode_i guards on core_key0 and core_key2, and with the analogous pattern in aes0_wrapper. An attacker or debugger with debug_mode_i asserted can still access the real AES key material stored in key_reg1 through the core_key path and via direct register reads.
finding.security_impact: AES key material stored in key slot 1 (key_reg1 / core_key1) is exposed to entities operating in debug mode. This breaks the intended isolation of cryptographic key material during debug sessions, potentially allowing extraction of symmetric keys used for AES encryption/decryption operations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet does not show how core_key1 is ultimately consumed by the AES core instantiation (the aes_core module connection is not in scope), but given that core_key is selected from core_key0/1/2 via key_sel (line 156) and fed to the AES core, the leakage path through the core is structurally confirmed. The read-side guard (reglk_ctrl_i[2]) on key_reg1 is present but orthogonal to the debug_mode_i zeroing gap.

finding.summary: core_key1 is assigned without the debug_mode_i guard that protects core_key0 and core_key2, allowing key material from key_reg1 to leak to the AES core even in debug mode.

evidence.description: Line 149 zeroes core_key0 when debug_mode_i is asserted, and line 153 zeroes core_key2 when debug_mode_i is asserted. However, line 151 assigns core_key1 directly from key_reg1 with NO debug_mode_i guard: 'assign core_key1 = {key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], ...};'. The missing conditional means the full 256-bit key material stored in key_reg1 is forwarded to core_key1 regardless of debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: core_key1 lacks the debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : ... protection present on core_key0 and core_key2, exposing key_reg1 contents to the AES core during debug mode.

evidence.description: core_key0 assignment at line 149: 'assign core_key0 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {key_reg0[7],...}' and core_key2 at line 153: 'assign core_key2 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {key_reg2[7],...}' both correctly zero the key output during debug mode. core_key1 (line 151) has no such guard.
evidence.supports_claim: The intended security pattern for debug mode is to zero key outputs; core_key1 omits this pattern.

evidence.description: key_reg1 is an 8-element 32-bit register array (line 119) holding one of the three selectable AES key slots. It feeds core_key1 which in turn feeds core_key (line 156) when key_sel[0] is asserted and key_sel[1] is deasserted.
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 is sensitive AES key material that should be protected from readback/use in debug mode, consistent with the treatment of key_reg0 and key_reg2.

evidence.description: The read-side logic at lines 292-294 protects key readback through reglk_ctrl_i[2], but the core_key1 signal is directly wired to the AES core (line 156). During debug mode with key_sel[0]=1 and key_sel[1]=0, the unguarded core_key1 value propagates to core_key and into AES encryption/decryption operations, leaking key_reg1.
evidence.supports_claim: An attacker with debug mode access can cause AES operations using key_reg1 material that should have been zeroed out.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with debug_mode_i connected from the system-level debug_mode_i signal (line 1282), confirming that the debug_mode_i input in aes1_wrapper is populated from an actual debug mode signal.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i signal reaching aes1_wrapper is a real system-level control, making the missing guard on core_key1 an exploitable condition during debug sessions.

finding.reasoning_summary: The aes1_wrapper implements a security policy where AES key registers are zeroed when forwarded to the AES core during debug mode. core_key0 (line 149) and core_key2 (line 153) both apply 'debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : key_reg_value'. core_key1 (line 151) is missing this conditional entirely, forwarding key_reg1 directly to the core. When key_sel selects key slot 1 (key_sel=2'b01), core_key1 becomes core_key, exposing secret key material to the AES core during debug mode. This is an inconsistent application of the debug-mode protection policy.
finding.security_impact: During debug mode, the AES key stored in key_reg1 is not zeroed before being fed to the AES core. An attacker with debug access can perform AES encryption/decryption operations using the secret key material in key_reg1, or observe side-channel effects, effectively bypassing the intended debug isolation for that key slot.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is confirmed from source snippets that the debug_mode_i guard is absent on core_key1. No additional ambiguity exists regarding the code path. The only minor uncertainty is whether key_sel can be forced to select key1 during debug mode, but since key_sel is reset to 0 and writable via reglk_ctrl_i[5] control, an attacker in debug mode with write access could set it.

finding.summary: AES1 wrapper passes reglk_ctrl to the AES1 core without the 'we_flag' OR-mask that AES0 wrapper receives, creating an inconsistent lock protection. Additionally, key material (key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2) can be read back via the rdata path when reglk_ctrl_i[2] is not set, and debug_mode_i zeroes only core_key0 and core_key2 while leaving core_key1 always exposed to the AES core regardless of debug mode.

evidence.description: AES0 wrapper instantiation ORs reglk_ctrl with we_flag_1: reglk_ctrl_i ( reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1 ), providing an additional write-enable override into the lock signal.
evidence.supports_claim: AES0 has an additional we_flag protection layer that AES1 lacks, showing inconsistent guard application.

evidence.description: AES1 wrapper instantiation passes reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8] directly without any we_flag OR: .reglk_ctrl_i ( reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8]). The we_flag override present for AES0 is absent here.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing we_flag OR means the register lock for AES1 can be bypassed in scenarios where we_flag_1 would have locked AES0, creating asymmetric protection.

evidence.description: Key registers key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2 are readable via rdata when the corresponding reglk_ctrl_i[2] bit is 0. Line 292: 'if (key_we0) rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[2] ? 32'b0 : key_reg0[address[5:3]]'. Key readback is gated only by reglk_ctrl_i[2], with no additional access control check.
evidence.supports_claim: AES key material is exposed over the AXI read interface when the register lock bit is not set, and there is no separate privilege or access control gate on key readback in the read path shown.

evidence.description: core_key1 assignment at line 151-152: 'assign core_key1 = {key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], ...}' has no debug_mode_i guard, while core_key0 and core_key2 are zeroed in debug mode (lines 149, 153). This means key_reg1 contents are always forwarded to the AES core even in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Asymmetric debug-mode protection: key1 is not zeroed in debug mode unlike key0 and key2, allowing key1 material to reach the AES core even when debug_mode_i is asserted.

evidence.description: Write-side lock checks in aes1_wrapper use reglk_ctrl_i bits to conditionally update registers (e.g., keylen_reg0 <= reglk_ctrl_i[3] ? keylen_reg0 : wdata). The lock check is present for writes but the inconsistency with we_flag missing (as in AES0) remains.
evidence.supports_claim: Write-side lock logic exists but is incomplete relative to AES0's additional we_flag layer.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippets show two concrete vulnerabilities: (1) AES1's reglk_ctrl_i connection omits the we_flag OR-mask that AES0 receives, creating asymmetric register lock protection. (2) The debug_mode_i guard on core_key is applied to core_key0 and core_key2 but not core_key1, meaning key1 material is always forwarded to the AES core regardless of debug mode. Additionally, key readback via rdata is gated only by a single reglk_ctrl bit with no defense-in-depth.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can manipulate the privilege level or the we_flag signal can bypass register locks on AES1 that would have been enforced on AES0. The unguarded core_key1 path allows AES key material to reach the cryptographic core even in debug mode, potentially enabling side-channel extraction or unauthorized decryption. Key readback through the AXI read interface is possible whenever reglk_ctrl_i[2] is not set.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact definition and source of we_flag_1 and whether it is wired into AES1 through another path is not shown in the provided snippets. The full reglk_wrapper logic that sets reglk_ctrl bits is not included, so the runtime conditions under which reglk_ctrl_i[2] and [3] are cleared cannot be fully assessed.

finding.summary: AES1 wrapper connects reglk_ctrl_i without the we_flag OR-mask that AES0 uses, and key_reg1 readback in aes1_wrapper is not gated by any reglk_ctrl_i bit, allowing unrestricted read of key material.

evidence.description: AES0 instantiation applies 'reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1' to reglk_ctrl_i, ensuring a write-flag can unconditionally lock the registers as an additional safety measure.
evidence.supports_claim: AES0 has an extra OR with we_flag_1 in its reglk_ctrl_i connection, providing stronger protection.

evidence.description: AES1 instantiation passes 'reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8]' directly to reglk_ctrl_i with no OR of any we_flag. Unlike AES0, there is no additional lock enforcement, meaning if reglk_ctrl bits are zero the registers are fully writable/readable.
evidence.supports_claim: AES1 is missing the we_flag OR-mask applied to AES0, creating an asymmetric and weaker protection state.

evidence.description: Read-side case statement gates core_result on reglk_ctrl_i[4] and key_reg0 on reglk_ctrl_i[2], but key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback is not shown to be gated. Lines 292+ show key_reg0 gated, but the snippet ends there without equivalent protection for key_reg1/key_reg2.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive AES key material in key_reg1 and key_reg2 may be readable without a reglk_ctrl guard.

evidence.description: core_result is a 128-bit AES output register. It is declared here and later gated only by reglk_ctrl_i[4] in the read path. If reglk_ctrl_i is zero (not locked), core_result is directly readable.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES result register is a sensitive output that depends entirely on reglk_ctrl_i being set correctly.

evidence.description: Write-side register update for block_reg uses reglk_ctrl_i[5], and key registers use reglk_ctrl_i[3]/[5]. These guards are present but contingent on reglk_ctrl_i being non-zero, which is not enforced at the instantiation site for AES1.
evidence.supports_claim: All write-side protections in AES1 depend on reglk_ctrl_i being set; the instantiation does not add a fallback lock like AES0 does.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper drives reglk_ctrl_o, which feeds into riscv_peripherals.reglk_ctrl. If reglk_mem is zero at reset (default), all peripheral register lock bits are zero, leaving all peripherals including AES1 unlocked.
evidence.supports_claim: Default zero state of reglk registers means all reglk_ctrl_i inputs are zero at reset, disabling all register lock protections until explicitly programmed.

finding.reasoning_summary: The AES1 wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i directly from reglk_ctrl without the additional OR-mask (we_flag) applied to AES0. At reset, reglk_mem in reglk_wrapper defaults to zero, making all reglk_ctrl_i bits zero. With all lock bits zero, the read path in aes1_wrapper returns core_result, key registers, and other sensitive state unmasked. Additionally, key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback does not appear to be guarded by any reglk_ctrl_i bit in the visible snippet, unlike key_reg0 which is gated by reglk_ctrl_i[2]. This creates a window from reset until software explicitly programs the lock registers where AES key material and encryption results are fully readable and writable.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with bus access before reglk registers are programmed can read AES encryption results (core_result) and potentially AES key material (key_reg1, key_reg2) from aes1_wrapper, and can also overwrite key and control registers, compromising the confidentiality and integrity of AES operations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet for the key_reg1/key_reg2 read path is truncated at line 292; there may be additional guards not visible in the provided snippet. Also, the exact reset value of reglk_mem is not shown in the provided reglk_wrapper snippet. The we_flag mechanism and its effect on AES1 specifically is inferred from the asymmetry between SNIP-0003 and SNIP-0004.

finding.summary: In aes1_wrapper, core_key1 is assigned the raw key register contents without any debug_mode_i zeroing guard, unlike core_key0 and core_key2 which are masked when debug_mode_i is asserted. This asymmetric protection allows key material from key_reg1 to leak to the AES core even when the CPU is in debug mode.

evidence.description: Line 149 assigns core_key0 as 'debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {key_reg0[...]}' and line 153 assigns core_key2 as 'debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {key_reg2[...]}', but line 151 assigns core_key1 as '{key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], ...}' with NO debug_mode_i guard. The key stored in key_reg1 is unconditionally passed to the AES core regardless of debug mode state.
evidence.supports_claim: Asymmetric debug-mode protection: core_key1 is never zeroed in debug mode, exposing key_reg1 to the AES core and potentially to a debug-mode attacker.

evidence.description: In aes0_wrapper, key_big0 and key_big1 are both masked with 'debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 : ...' (lines 54-55), while key_big2 at line 56 has no such guard. This pattern of incomplete masking is consistent with the vulnerability seen in aes1_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: Establishes the intended design pattern of zeroing key outputs in debug mode, making the missing guard on core_key1 in aes1_wrapper a deviation and vulnerability.

evidence.description: In hmac_wrapper, line 54 assigns 'key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[0],...}', properly zeroing the key in debug mode. This further confirms the intended security pattern and that the missing guard on aes1_wrapper's core_key1 is an oversight.
evidence.supports_claim: Cross-module evidence that debug_mode_i is supposed to zero sensitive key material; aes1_wrapper's core_key1 omits this protection.

evidence.description: The riscv_peripherals module instantiates aes1_wrapper and directly connects the top-level debug_mode_i signal to it (line 1282), confirming that debug_mode_i is an externally-controlled input that determines when key masking should apply.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is a real privilege/mode signal from outside the module, making the missing guard on core_key1 an exploitable vulnerability in debug mode.

evidence.description: The aes1_wrapper register-update logic (lines 235-239) shows key_reg0, key_reg1, and key_reg2 are all loaded from AXI writes. The core_key mux at line 156 selects among core_key0, core_key1, core_key2 based on key_sel, meaning core_key1 (unguarded) can be the active key used in AES operations.
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 content is a real AES key that can be used in cryptographic operations; its unguarded exposure via core_key1 in debug mode is a concrete key leakage path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The design intends to zero out sensitive key material when debug_mode_i is asserted, as evidenced by consistent patterns in aes0_wrapper (key_big0, key_big1) and hmac_wrapper (key). In aes1_wrapper, core_key0 and core_key2 are correctly guarded with debug_mode_i, but core_key1 is assigned directly from key_reg1 without any debug_mode_i masking. Since the core_key mux can select core_key1 as the active AES key, this allows the key stored in key_reg1 to be used by the AES core even when debug mode is active. This is an incomplete/asymmetric implementation of the debug-mode key protection policy.
finding.security_impact: When the CPU enters debug mode, an attacker with debug access can trigger AES operations using the unmasked core_key1 key material. This defeats the debug-mode key isolation security policy, potentially allowing exfiltration of secret key material from key_reg1 through cryptographic oracle behavior or direct side-channel observation during debug sessions.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown in the snippets whether key_sel can be externally set to select core_key1 while in debug mode, but the mux logic is combinational and key_sel is a writable register (see SNIP-0014 line 230/242), so this is expected to be controllable. The read-path behavior of key_reg1 in debug mode is also not shown but is a secondary concern given the write-path exposure.

finding.summary: The aes1_wrapper init_reg and key registers are protected by reglk_ctrl_i bits during normal writes, but the key material (key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2) and AES result registers are readable back via the rdata path with only a single reglk_ctrl_i bit check, and critically, the key write-enable signals (key_we0/1/2) are derived purely from address decoding without any reglk_ctrl_i guard on the write path for key registers.

evidence.description: init_reg write is guarded by reglk_ctrl_i[1], block_reg write by reglk_ctrl_i[5], encdec_reg by reglk_ctrl_i[1], keylen by reglk_ctrl_i[3]. However, the key register write path (key_we0/1/2) is set purely by address decoding in SNIP-0017 without any reglk_ctrl_i check in the write clause shown here. The case statement in the always block does not include a case for KEY addresses to apply reglk_ctrl_i gating on key_reg writes.
evidence.supports_claim: Key register writes (key_reg0/1/2) are not protected by reglk_ctrl_i in the write logic, allowing key material to be overwritten even when the register lock is asserted.

evidence.description: key_we0, key_we1, key_we2 are assigned purely from address range comparisons with no reglk_ctrl_i qualification. These signals directly gate writes to key_reg0/1/2 without checking whether the register lock is active.
evidence.supports_claim: The key write-enable signals are not gated by reglk_ctrl_i, so when en && we is true and the address falls in the key range, key material can be written regardless of the lock state.

evidence.description: Key readback at line 292 uses reglk_ctrl_i[2] to zero out key_reg0 on read. AES result registers use reglk_ctrl_i[4]. However, the read protection is a separate concern from the missing write protection on key registers.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key and result registers have read guards, confirming intent to protect them, making the missing write guard on key registers a clear inconsistency.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i driven by reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8] without any additional OR with a we_flag (unlike aes0_wrapper which ORs with we_flag_1), meaning the lock value passed in is exactly as configured by the reglk module.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl signal is properly routed to aes1_wrapper, so the missing guard inside aes1_wrapper on key writes is the vulnerability rather than an upstream issue.

evidence.description: key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2 are declared as 256-bit key material registers. Their write path is controlled by key_we0/1/2 signals that are purely address-decoded without lock checking.
evidence.supports_claim: AES key registers are sensitive assets whose write path is insufficiently protected by the register lock mechanism.

finding.reasoning_summary: In aes1_wrapper, the write-side register update logic (SNIP-0018) applies reglk_ctrl_i checks for init_reg, next_reg, encdec_reg, keylen_reg, block_reg, and key_sel. However, the actual key register arrays (key_reg0/1/2) are written when key_we0/1/2 are asserted (SNIP-0017), which are purely address-decoded combinational signals with no reglk_ctrl_i gating. The SNIP-0018 case statement handles ADDR_CTRL, CTRL_ENCDEC, CTRL_KEYLEN*, and KEY_SEL with lock checks, but does not include a case for the KEY address range to check reglk_ctrl_i[2] (the bit used on the read side). The key_reg write block presumably exists outside the case (or falls through to the if(key_we*) block) without a lock check, allowing key material to be written even when the register lock is engaged.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged software that can issue AXI write transactions to the aes1_wrapper can overwrite AES key registers (key_reg0/1/2) even when the register lock (reglk_ctrl_i) is asserted, defeating the intended key protection mechanism and enabling key substitution attacks.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet SNIP-0018 shows lines 226-260 but the key_reg write logic (the if(key_we0)/if(key_we1)/if(key_we2) assignments) is not fully shown in the provided snippets. The conclusion that key writes lack a reglk guard is based on the absence of such a check in the visible code and the pattern of explicit guards on all other registers; the full always block body after line 260 is not provided.

finding.summary: AES1 key register key_reg1 is not zeroed on debug_mode_i, allowing key material readback in debug mode, while core_key1 lacks the debug-mode suppression applied to core_key0 and core_key2.

evidence.description: key_reg0, key_reg1, and key_reg2 are the three AES key register banks in aes1_wrapper. They hold the AES cryptographic key material.
evidence.supports_claim: These registers are the sensitive resources that must be protected from unauthorized readback.

evidence.description: core_key0 is assigned as 'debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {key_reg0[7]...key_reg0[0]}' and core_key2 is assigned as 'debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : {key_reg2[7]...key_reg2[0]}', zeroing them in debug mode. However, core_key1 is assigned as '{key_reg1[7]...key_reg1[0]}' with NO debug_mode_i guard, meaning key_reg1 content flows unconditionally to core_key1.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug-mode suppression is inconsistently applied: key slot 1 (core_key1) is never zeroed in debug mode, constituting a missing guard.

evidence.description: The read side of aes1_wrapper at line 292 returns key_reg0 content when key_we0 is asserted, gated by reglk_ctrl_i[2]. There is no debug_mode_i check on the read path for key registers, and the write-enable signals key_we0/key_we1/key_we2 are computed purely from the address (SNIP-0016) with no reglk or debug guard. Additionally, core_key1 flows to the AES core without debug suppression.
evidence.supports_claim: Key register content can be read back through the register interface, and core_key1 is forwarded to the AES core without zeroing in debug mode, exposing key material.

evidence.description: key_we0, key_we1, key_we2 are computed purely as address-range comparisons with no reference to reglk_ctrl_i or debug_mode_i. The write-enable is not gated by any lock or privilege signal at the combinational level.
evidence.supports_claim: Write enables for key registers lack any protection check at the combinational level, meaning the reglk protection is only applied inside the sequential write block.

evidence.description: Key write logic inside the always_ff block does not include a case for key_reg writes protected by reglk_ctrl_i. The key registers (key_reg0/1/2) are written via separate 'if (key_we0)' style blocks not shown to check reglk for key_reg1 writes specifically, and the reset path zeros all key registers unconditionally on rst_2.
evidence.supports_claim: The sequential logic does not show a reglk guard for key_reg1 writes, consistent with an asymmetric protection implementation.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with reglk_ctrl_i driven from reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8] and debug_mode_i passed directly from the top-level debug_mode_i port. No additional privilege check is interposed.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i reaches aes1_wrapper without any additional filtering, so the asymmetric debug suppression bug directly affects system security.

finding.reasoning_summary: In aes1_wrapper, three key register banks (key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2) hold AES key material. When debug_mode_i is asserted, core_key0 and core_key2 are zeroed before being forwarded to the AES core, but core_key1 is assigned directly from key_reg1 with no debug_mode_i guard (SNIP-0015, lines 149-153). This means that in debug mode, key slot 1 is still forwarded to the AES core. Furthermore, the read path (SNIP-0018) allows key_reg0 to be read back when reglk_ctrl_i[2] is not set, but there is no debug_mode_i suppression on the read side for any key register. The combination of an inconsistent debug suppression on the output path and a readable key register interface constitutes a concrete permission-security violation.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or firmware running in debug mode can recover AES key material stored in key_reg1 by using the AES core with a known plaintext (since core_key1 is not zeroed) or by directly reading key_reg0 through the register interface if reglk_ctrl_i[2] is not set. This undermines the confidentiality of all AES operations that use key slot 1.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full key_reg1 write-path sequential logic with potential reglk guard is not shown in the snippets (only a partial view of the always_ff block at SNIP-0017). However, the missing debug_mode_i guard on core_key1 assignment is directly visible in SNIP-0015 and constitutes a concrete, standalone finding.

finding.summary: AES1 wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i without the 'we_flag' OR-mask applied, unlike AES0 which OR-merges a sticky write-enable flag into the lock bits, creating an inconsistency. More critically, SNIP-0018 shows that key readback (key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2) in the read path uses only reglk_ctrl_i[2] as a guard for key_reg0, while key_reg1 reads are not shown to have any lock guard in the same snippet, and SNIP-0015 reveals that core_key1 is assigned directly from key_reg1 without the debug_mode_i zero-out that protects core_key0 and core_key2, allowing key material from key_reg1 to leak regardless of debug mode.

evidence.description: AES1 wrapper instantiation passes reglk_ctrl_i as reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8] with NO we_flag OR-mask, unlike AES0 (SNIP-0003) which uses '| we_flag_1'.
evidence.supports_claim: AES1's register-lock input is weaker than AES0's because the sticky write-enable override is not applied.

evidence.description: AES0 instantiation applies 'reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1' as the lock value, providing an additional sticky lock bit not present for AES1.
evidence.supports_claim: Demonstrates by contrast that AES1 is missing the we_flag OR-hardening applied to AES0.

evidence.description: core_key1 is assigned directly as '{key_reg1[7],...,key_reg1[0]}' without any debug_mode_i zeroing, while core_key0 and core_key2 are conditionally zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted. This means key_reg1 content is always forwarded to the AES core even in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Key material in key_reg1 is not suppressed during debug mode, breaking the debug isolation applied to key_reg0 and key_reg2.

evidence.description: Read path returns key_reg0 guarded by reglk_ctrl_i[2], but key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback cases are not visible in the snippet, suggesting incomplete or missing lock guards for key_reg1/key_reg2 readback paths.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive AES key material may be readable without proper lock enforcement for all key register banks.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i is a module input to aes1_wrapper and is used selectively to zero out core_key0 and core_key2 but not core_key1, creating an asymmetric debug protection.
evidence.supports_claim: Incomplete debug mode key suppression allows key_reg1 to remain accessible to the AES core during debug sessions.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities are present in aes1_wrapper. First, AES1's reglk_ctrl_i is connected without the 'we_flag' OR-hardening that AES0 receives, meaning once an attacker writes certain registers the sticky lock override is absent. Second and more severe, the debug_mode_i zeroing logic applied to core_key0 and core_key2 is entirely absent for core_key1 (line 151 vs lines 149-150 and 153), so key material stored in key_reg1 always reaches the AES core even when debug mode is active. This is directly evidenced by SNIP-0015 showing the asymmetric assignments.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access can extract or influence AES encryption/decryption using the key material from key_reg1 which is not zeroed in debug mode, potentially enabling key recovery or controlled-plaintext attacks. Additionally, the missing we_flag hardening on AES1's lock control means register-lock protections may be bypassable under conditions where the flag-based override would have applied.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-path case statement for key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback is not shown in the available snippets (SNIP-0018 ends at line 292 and shows only key_reg0 guard). The we_flag_1 definition and its exact semantics are not shown. However, the debug_mode_i asymmetry for core_key1 is unambiguously present in SNIP-0015.

finding.summary: In aes1_wrapper, core_key1 (key_reg1) is assigned without any debug_mode_i zeroing guard, while core_key0 and core_key2 are protected. This means key_reg1 leaks its full value to the AES core even in debug mode, creating an inconsistent and bypassable key-protection policy.

evidence.description: Lines 149-154: core_key0 is zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted ('assign core_key0 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : ...'), and core_key2 is similarly zeroed ('assign core_key2 = debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : ...'). However, core_key1 (line 151-152) has NO debug_mode_i guard and unconditionally exposes key_reg1 to the AES core.
evidence.supports_claim: The protection applied to key0 and key2 is missing for key1, making the debug-mode key-zeroing protection incomplete and bypassable via key1.

evidence.description: Line 151: 'assign core_key1 = {key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], key_reg1[5], key_reg1[4], key_reg1[3], key_reg1[2], key_reg1[1], key_reg1[0]};' — no conditional zeroing on debug_mode_i, unlike core_key0 and core_key2 which both have 'debug_mode_i ? 'b0 : ...' guards.
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 is unconditionally passed to the AES core regardless of debug_mode_i, violating the intended debug-mode key confidentiality policy.

evidence.description: Lines 292+: Key registers (key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2) are readable back via the register interface gated by reglk_ctrl_i[2]. The AES core key inputs (core_key0, core_key1, core_key2) are the cryptographic key material used for encryption/decryption operations.
evidence.supports_claim: The key material in key_reg1 is a sensitive protected resource that should be zeroed in debug mode but is not.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper lines 54-56 show the same pattern: key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but key_big2 is NOT. This confirms a recurring inconsistency across AES wrapper modules where the debug-mode key-zeroing protection is partially applied.
evidence.supports_claim: The incomplete debug_mode_i guard pattern appears across multiple AES wrappers, indicating a systemic security design flaw rather than an isolated oversight.

finding.reasoning_summary: The aes1_wrapper is designed to zero out cryptographic key material when debug_mode_i is asserted, preventing key leakage during debug/test operations. This protection is applied to core_key0 and core_key2 but is entirely absent for core_key1. An attacker with debug mode access can obtain key_reg1 through the AES core's operation or by observing core_key1. Additionally, in aes0_wrapper, key_big2 similarly lacks the debug_mode_i guard. These omissions mean the debug-mode key confidentiality protection is only partial, and a multi-key AES configuration using key1 (or key2 in aes0) would leak that key material in debug mode.
finding.security_impact: In debug mode, key_reg1 (a 256-bit AES key) is unconditionally forwarded to the AES core. An attacker who can assert debug_mode_i and trigger AES operations, or who can observe the core_key1 signal, can recover a full AES key that should have been protected. This undermines the key confidentiality guarantees of the register-lock and debug-mode protection scheme.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-back path for key_reg1 in debug mode via the register interface is not fully shown in the snippets (only key_reg0 readback is shown at line 292). However, the missing debug_mode_i guard on core_key1 assignment is unambiguous from the source code. The impact depends on whether an attacker can indirectly recover key material from AES core outputs when key1 is used.

finding.summary: In aes1_wrapper, key_reg1 is readable without any reglk_ctrl guard and key_reg1 is also not zeroed in debug mode, while key_reg0 and key_reg2 are individually protected. Additionally, aes0_wrapper's key2 is not zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, unlike key0 and key1.

evidence.description: core_key0 and core_key2 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but core_key1 is assigned directly from key_reg1 without any debug_mode_i guard: 'assign core_key1 = {key_reg1[7], key_reg1[6], ...}' at line 151.
evidence.supports_claim: key_reg1 is not zeroed during debug mode, allowing the AES key material in key_reg1 to be used by the core even when debug_mode_i is active.

evidence.description: Read-side protection at lines 292+ uses 'if (key_we0) rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[2] ? 32'b0 : key_reg0[address[5:3]]' showing key_reg0 has a lock guard, but there is no corresponding read-back guard shown for key_reg1 in the provided read path.
evidence.supports_claim: The read protection pattern is inconsistently applied across key register banks in aes1_wrapper.

evidence.description: In aes0_wrapper, key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is set ('debug_mode_i ? 192'b0'), but key_big2 at line 56 is assigned unconditionally: 'assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};' with no debug_mode_i guard.
evidence.supports_claim: key2 in aes0_wrapper leaks key material to the AES core even when debug_mode_i is asserted, violating the intent of the debug protection pattern applied to key0 and key1.

evidence.description: key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2 are 256-bit AES key registers in aes1_wrapper storing cryptographic key material used directly by core_key.
evidence.supports_claim: These are sensitive cryptographic registers that require consistent protection in both debug mode and register-lock scenarios.

evidence.description: Write side for key registers uses key_we0/key_we1/key_we2 signals but the actual write to key_reg1/key_reg2 is not shown with explicit reglk_ctrl_i guards in the shown snippet, while encdec, keylen, block, and key_sel have explicit reglk_ctrl checks.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent application of reglk_ctrl_i guards across write paths suggests missing protection on key register writes.

evidence.description: In aes2_wrapper, cii_K2 at line 57 is assigned 'debug_mode_i ? 128'b0 : ...' suggesting the pattern exists, but comparing with aes0_wrapper SNIP-0003 line 56 shows key_big2 lacks this guard, confirming the omission is a bug not a design choice.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug zeroing of key2 was intended (as seen in aes2_wrapper) but was omitted in aes0_wrapper, confirming a concrete vulnerability in aes0_wrapper.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities are present: (1) In aes0_wrapper, key_big2 (derived from key2 registers) is passed to the AES core without zeroing when debug_mode_i is asserted, unlike key_big0 and key_big1 which are both explicitly zeroed. This allows key material to leak through the crypto core in debug mode. (2) In aes1_wrapper, core_key1 is similarly not zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, while core_key0 and core_key2 are. These are asymmetric omissions within the same debug protection scheme, constituting exploitable gaps.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access can extract AES key material (key2 in aes0_wrapper, key1 in aes1_wrapper) by driving AES encryption operations and observing outputs, since these key registers are not zeroed during debug mode unlike their peer registers. This undermines the cryptographic isolation the debug_mode_i signal is intended to enforce.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full write-side logic for key_reg1 and key_reg2 in aes1_wrapper was not shown in the snippets (only partial write logic visible in SNIP-0007). The read-side path for key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback was also only partially shown. However, the debug mode zeroing omission for core_key1 in aes1_wrapper and key_big2 in aes0_wrapper is clearly visible in the source snippets and is unambiguous.

finding.summary: In aes1_wrapper, key registers (key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2) are protected on write by reglk_ctrl_i bits, but the read-back path exposes key_reg0 and key_reg1 with inadequate or missing lock guards, and the write path for key_reg1 lacks any reglk_ctrl_i guard, allowing unprotected writes to one of the three AES key banks.

evidence.description: The read-back case for key_reg0 checks reglk_ctrl_i[2], but key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback paths are absent from the case statement shown. More critically, line 292 shows 'if (key_we0) rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[2] ? 32'b0 : key_reg0[address[5:3]]' — key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback are not shown as guarded, allowing potential sensitive key material readback without lock.
evidence.supports_claim: Read-back of key registers is incompletely guarded: only key_reg0 read is shown with a reglk_ctrl_i check; key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback guards are absent from the visible logic.

evidence.description: In the write path (lines 258-259), block_reg writes check reglk_ctrl_i[5]. Key writes for key_reg0/1/2 use key_we0/key_we1/key_we2 signals derived purely from address decoding (SNIP-0017), but the actual register update logic for key_reg1 and key_reg2 (which would appear after line 260) is not shown as gated by any reglk_ctrl_i bit in the visible snippet. Only encdec_reg, keylen_reg, init_reg, next_reg, key_sel, and block_reg show explicit reglk_ctrl_i guards; key_reg1 and key_reg2 write guards are not evidenced.
evidence.supports_claim: Key register banks key_reg1 and key_reg2 may lack write protection guards (reglk_ctrl_i checks) visible in the code, meaning writes to those registers are not locked by the register-lock control mechanism.

evidence.description: AES1 wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i driven by reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8], without the additional 'we_flag' OR that aes0_wrapper receives (compare SNIP-0004 which ORs 'we_flag_1'). This means the lock override mechanism available to aes0 is missing for aes1.
evidence.supports_claim: AES1's reglk_ctrl_i lacks the we_flag OR present in AES0, representing an inconsistency in the lock-override chain that may weaken protection.

evidence.description: key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2 are 256-bit AES key registers storing cryptographic key material. Insufficient write/read guards on these registers constitute a security risk allowing key extraction or unauthorized key injection.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES key registers are sensitive cryptographic assets requiring full protection by the reglk_ctrl_i mechanism.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl is a wide bus [8*NB_PERIPHERALS-1:0] controlling per-peripheral register lock bits. Individual bits gate writes and reads to sensitive registers. Misconfiguration or missing guards on specific bits allows bypass.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock control infrastructure exists, but its application to all key register read/write paths in aes1_wrapper is incomplete.

finding.reasoning_summary: The aes1_wrapper contains AES key registers split across three banks (key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2). The write path shows reglk_ctrl_i guards for control registers (encdec, keylen, init, next, key_sel, block) but the visible code does not show explicit reglk_ctrl_i guards for key_reg1 and key_reg2 writes. The read path (SNIP-0019) only shows a guard for key_reg0 readback (reglk_ctrl_i[2]); key_reg1 and key_reg2 readback paths are not guarded in the visible code. Additionally, aes1_wrapper's instantiation lacks the we_flag OR override that aes0_wrapper receives, creating an inconsistency. These gaps allow potential unauthorized read or write of AES cryptographic key material.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with bus access could read back AES key material from key_reg1 or key_reg2 without the register-lock being enforced, or inject unauthorized key values into those banks, compromising AES encryption/decryption confidentiality and integrity.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippet for the key_reg1 and key_reg2 write update logic (expected after line 260 in aes1_wrapper.sv) is not fully visible in the provided snippets — it is possible guards exist beyond the shown code window. Similarly, key_reg1/key_reg2 readback code may exist elsewhere. Confidence is medium rather than high due to this truncation.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper resets its access-control memory (acct_mem) to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF) on reset, which is the maximally permissive state. If a subsequent boot or partial reset occurs, all peripherals become fully accessible until software explicitly reprograms the access-control table.

evidence.description: On reset (when ~(rst_ni && ~rst_6) is true), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for every entry. This means all access-control bits are set to 1, granting maximum access permissions to every peripheral until explicitly restricted by software.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes the permission store to an all-permissive state rather than a deny-all default, creating a window of unrestricted access after any reset event.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is directly derived from acct_mem contents: assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}. When acct_mem holds 0xFFFFFFFF after reset, acc_ctrl_o exports all-ones, granting access to all downstream peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control output propagated to all peripherals reflects the insecure reset state of acct_mem.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl (which comes from acct_wrapper's acc_ctrl_o) and is fanned out to all security-critical peripheral wrappers (AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, REGLK). All-ones in acc_ctrl_c means all privilege levels have access to all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: The permissive reset state propagates through acc_ctrl_c to every protected peripheral in the system.

evidence.description: acct_ctrl_i for AES0 is driven by acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][1], meaning permission is granted when the corresponding bit is 1. After reset, this bit is 1, so any privilege level can access AES0.
evidence.supports_claim: All-ones in acc_ctrl_c after reset means every privilege level passes the access check for AES0.

evidence.description: acct_ctrl_i for AES2 is driven by acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][11]. After reset, acct_mem holds all-ones, so this bit is 1 and all privilege levels can access AES2 and its cryptographic key registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The permissive reset state grants all privilege levels access to the AES2 crypto peripheral including key material.

evidence.description: Line 84: acct_mem[j] <= 32'hffffffff initializes every access-control entry to all-ones on reset. A secure-by-default design should initialize to 0 (deny all) or to the minimum necessary privilege, requiring explicit unlock rather than explicit lock.
evidence.supports_claim: Insecure reset-to-all-ones violates the principle of least privilege; any reset event leaves the system fully open until software intervention.

evidence.description: AES2 wrapper contains cryptographic key registers (key0, key1, key2) and cipher state. With acct_ctrl_i=1 after reset, any bus master at any privilege level can read/write these registers before the access-control table is locked down.
evidence.supports_claim: The all-permissive reset state exposes sensitive cryptographic key material to unauthorized access during the boot window.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module stores per-peripheral access-control entries in acct_mem. On any reset event, all entries are initialized to 32'hffffffff, setting every access-control bit to 1. This value is immediately exported as acc_ctrl_o, which flows through riscv_peripherals as acc_ctrl_c and is used as the acct_ctrl_i gating signal for every security-sensitive peripheral (AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, REGLK). Because the access-control gate is 1 for all privilege levels immediately after reset, there is a window during which any bus master can access cryptographic peripherals and their key registers without restriction. This violates secure-by-default principles and represents a concrete permission-related vulnerability.
finding.security_impact: During the post-reset boot window before software programs the access-control table, all privilege levels have unrestricted read/write access to AES, HMAC, and SHA256 peripherals, including their cryptographic key registers. An unprivileged or malicious process executing early in boot could exfiltrate or overwrite cryptographic keys.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The duration of the vulnerable window depends on boot software behavior (how quickly it programs acct_mem). The snippets for aes1_wrapper en assignment and the exact gating logic inside each peripheral's write path were not fully shown, but the pattern is consistent across all wrappers visible in the snippets.

finding.summary: In aes0_wrapper, the read-side logic exposes key material directly when no lock bit is set, but the default rdata assignment at line 167 unconditionally reads key0[address[8:3]] before the case statement can override it, meaning key register contents leak for any unhandled address. More critically, in aes1_wrapper the key readback at lines 292-294 is only gated by reglk_ctrl_i[2] but not by debug_mode_i, allowing key registers to be read back in debug mode despite debug_mode_i zeroing the key forwarded to the crypto core. Additionally, aes2_wrapper receives debug_mode_i (line 22) and uses it to zero the key outputs to the cipher core (lines 55-57), but the register write path (lines 112-138) does not check debug_mode_i at all—only reglk_ctrl_i—meaning a debug-mode agent can still write to key registers. The hmac_wrapper similarly zeros the key forwarded to the hash core under debug_mode_i (line 54) but does not prevent key register readback via reglk_ctrl_i alone.

evidence.description: In aes0_wrapper read logic, line 167 assigns rdata = key0[address[8:3]] unconditionally before the case statement. For any address not covered by the case, raw key0 contents are returned without any reglk_ctrl_i or debug_mode_i guard.
evidence.supports_claim: Default rdata assignment leaks key0 register contents for unmapped addresses.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper zeroes key_big0 and key_big1 when debug_mode_i is asserted (lines 54-55), protecting the key forwarded to the AES core. However, key_big2 (line 56) is NOT gated by debug_mode_i, so key2 is always forwarded to the core regardless of debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: key2 leaks to the AES core in debug mode because line 56 lacks a debug_mode_i guard.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper zeroes core_key0 and core_key2 under debug_mode_i (lines 149, 153), but core_key1 (line 151) is NOT gated by debug_mode_i, leaking key_reg1 to the AES core in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: core_key1 is always forwarded to the AES core regardless of debug_mode_i.

evidence.description: aes1_wrapper key register readback at lines 292-294 is gated only by reglk_ctrl_i[2], not by debug_mode_i. A debug-mode requester with reglk_ctrl_i[2]=0 can read back raw key register values even though debug_mode_i is supposed to protect keys.
evidence.supports_claim: Key readback in debug mode is not blocked; debug_mode_i is absent from the read-path guard.

evidence.description: aes2_wrapper zeroes cii_K0, cii_K1, cii_K2 when debug_mode_i is asserted (lines 55-57), protecting the key forwarded to the cipher core.
evidence.supports_claim: Key output to cipher is protected in debug mode in aes2_wrapper.
