evidence.supports_claim: Unrestricted DMI access enables full CPU takeover including memory reads, register dumps, and arbitrary code execution via debug program buffer.

finding.reasoning_summary: The RTL instantiates SiFive's SimDTM, a DPI-based simulation component, directly in riscv_peripherals.sv. The module's output (debug_req_bits_op) is cast and assigned to the DMI request operation field without any intervening authentication, privilege level check, lifecycle state guard, or synthesis-time conditional compile guard visible in the provided source. This means the simulation debug backdoor may be present in a synthesized netlist, and even in simulation it operates without access controls. Any entity able to drive the DPI interface can issue arbitrary DMI transactions to halt processors, read/write memory, or execute code via the debug program buffer.
finding.security_impact: Full system compromise: an attacker with access to the simulation environment (or if the SimDTM is synthesized into silicon) can halt all RISC-V cores, read and write arbitrary physical memory, dump register files, and execute arbitrary code via the debug abstract command/program buffer interface—all without authentication.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet does not show whether a `ifdef SIMULATION or synthesis exclusion pragma wraps this block outside the shown line range (lines 247-264). If such a guard exists earlier in the file or at module level, the synthesis risk is mitigated, though the lack of DMI authentication remains a simulation security concern. Additionally, whether debug_req_valid is gated by any enable signal not shown here is unknown.

finding.summary: DMA AXI request protection (prot) fields are permanently tied to zero, disabling AXI bus-level privilege, security, and instruction/data access type signaling for all DMA transactions.

evidence.description: Lines 1963 and 1974 unconditionally assign dma_axi_req.aw.prot and dma_axi_req.ar.prot to 3'b000. The AXI4 PROT field encodes three protection attributes: privileged vs. unprivileged access (bit 0), secure vs. non-secure access (bit 1), and instruction vs. data access (bit 2). Forcing all bits to zero means every DMA transaction is permanently marked as unprivileged, non-secure, and data access regardless of the actual DMA operation context.
evidence.supports_claim: The PROT field is hardcoded to zero on both write (aw.prot) and read (ar.prot) channels, eliminating the bus-level security and privilege signaling that downstream interconnects, memory protection units, or TrustZone-aware slaves may rely upon to enforce access control.

evidence.description: The dma_axi_req structure carries AXI4 request signals including aw (write address) and ar (read address) channels with their associated protection attributes. These signals govern how downstream AXI slaves and interconnect components enforce memory protection and privilege checks on DMA-initiated bus transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: DMA AXI transactions that should carry elevated privilege or secure-world attributes will instead present as unprivileged non-secure transactions, potentially bypassing protection checks in downstream components that filter on PROT bits.

evidence.description: The comment at line 1956 states these are 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite'. However, prot is a standard AXI4 field used by interconnects and slaves for access control decisions. Treating it as a non-functional tie-off removes any bus-level permission checking for DMA traffic.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no conditional logic, privilege-level input, or security-mode input feeding into the prot field; it is unconditionally suppressed, constituting a missing guard.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet shows that dma_axi_req.aw.prot and dma_axi_req.ar.prot are both permanently assigned to zero via unconditional continuous assigns described as 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite'. The AXI4 PROT field carries security-critical information: bit 1 distinguishes secure from non-secure access (relevant to TrustZone or similar security partitioning), and bit 0 distinguishes privileged from unprivileged access. By hardcoding these to zero, all DMA bus transactions will appear as unprivileged, non-secure, data accesses. Downstream interconnect components or memory slaves that use PROT-based filtering to enforce access control boundaries will not be able to distinguish DMA traffic from ordinary unprivileged user-mode accesses, and will not block DMA from accessing secure or privileged memory regions based on these signals.
finding.security_impact: DMA transactions may bypass TrustZone-style secure/non-secure partitioning and privilege-level enforcement implemented in AXI interconnects or slaves, because the PROT signals that encode security and privilege state are always zero. This could allow the DMA engine to read from or write to secure memory regions or privileged-only resources that should be inaccessible to non-secure, unprivileged bus masters.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual downstream interconnect and slave behavior when receiving PROT=0 is not visible in this chain; some systems may not use PROT-based filtering at all, which would reduce impact. Additionally, the DMA engine itself may only ever perform operations that are legitimately non-secure and unprivileged, in which case the hardcoding may be intentional and benign. The AXI-lite bridging context noted in the comment suggests the DMA may operate through an AXI-lite adapter that does not propagate PROT, but the dma_axi_req signal itself appears to be a full AXI4 interface.

finding.summary: The reglk_wrapper reset condition includes jtag_unlock as a reset trigger, allowing JTAG to clear all register-lock memory (reglk_mem) back to zero, which disables all peripheral access controls enforced by reglk_ctrl_o.

evidence.description: The reset condition on line 80 is: `if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9))`. This means that asserting jtag_unlock (logic high) causes the entire reglk_mem array to be zeroed (lines 82-84). Since reglk_mem drives reglk_ctrl_o (the register lock output), clearing it to zero disables all register locks for all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: jtag_unlock signal acts as a full reset of all register-lock state, bypassing the protection enforced by reglk_ctrl registers for all downstream peripherals.

evidence.description: reglk_mem (line 39) stores the lock values, and reglk_ctrl_o (line 48) is directly derived from reglk_mem. Zeroing reglk_mem via jtag_unlock clears all lock bits, removing protection from all peripherals connected to reglk_ctrl_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock state that controls access to AES, SHA256, HMAC, and ACCT peripherals is fully controlled by reglk_mem, which is cleared by jtag_unlock.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] as its register lock input. If jtag_unlock clears reglk_mem, this field becomes 0, removing all register locks on AES0.
evidence.supports_claim: Downstream peripherals lose their protection when reglk_mem is zeroed by jtag_unlock.

evidence.description: The reglk_wrapper instance in riscv_peripherals connects jtag_unlock directly to the reglk_wrapper module (line 1817 region, edge:connects:54), confirming jtag_unlock from the top-level flows into the register lock reset logic.
evidence.supports_claim: jtag_unlock is an external input that reaches the register lock reset condition without any privilege or authentication guard in the RTL shown.

evidence.description: The write guard `en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i` (line 72) only gates normal writes; it does not protect against the jtag_unlock reset path. The reset branch (line 80) is entirely separate from the write-enable check, so acct_ctrl_i does not prevent jtag_unlock from clearing reglk_mem.
evidence.supports_claim: The access-control guard (acct_ctrl_i) does not protect the jtag_unlock reset path, leaving reglk_mem clearable by jtag_unlock unconditionally.

finding.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper uses jtag_unlock in its reset condition (line 80: `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`), meaning asserting jtag_unlock at any time forces all reglk_mem entries to zero. Since reglk_mem directly drives reglk_ctrl_o (the register lock output consumed by all security-sensitive peripherals including AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, and ACCT), this effectively disables all register-level access controls. No privilege check, authentication, or other guard is applied to the jtag_unlock signal before it reaches this reset path.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can assert jtag_unlock to clear all register lock state in reglk_mem, removing access controls from all cryptographic peripherals (AES, SHA256, HMAC, ACCT). This allows unauthorized reading or writing of sensitive registers in these peripherals, potentially exposing cryptographic keys or allowing manipulation of cryptographic operations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of the jtag_unlock signal (how it is asserted from the JTAG interface and whether any authentication is required at the JTAG level before jtag_unlock is driven high) is not visible in the provided snippets. However, the RTL clearly shows no guard on jtag_unlock within reglk_wrapper itself.

finding.summary: The AXI `lock` field is hardwired to zero ('0) for all peripheral AXI request buses (rst, rsa, rng, aes0), bypassing any exclusive-access or locked-transfer protection that the AXI protocol offers. This means no initiator can issue an exclusive or locked AXI transaction to the security-sensitive peripherals (AES, RSA, RNG, SHA256, HMAC, ACCT, REGLK), silently stripping an intended AXI protection attribute across all crypto and access-control peripherals.

evidence.description: rst_axi_req.aw.lock and rst_axi_req.ar.lock are both hardwired to '0 (lines 990 and 1001). This permanently disables AXI locked/exclusive-access signalling for the RST peripheral path.
evidence.supports_claim: The lock field, which controls AXI exclusive or locked bus access, is unconditionally tied off to zero, removing any ability for the bus fabric to enforce locked-transaction semantics.

evidence.description: rsa_axi_req.aw.lock and rsa_axi_req.ar.lock are both hardwired to '0 (lines 1075 and 1086). The RSA wrapper receives no locked-access indication from the interconnect.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms the same pattern of unconditional lock=0 assignment extends to the RSA cryptographic peripheral.

evidence.description: rng_axi_req.aw.lock and rng_axi_req.ar.lock are both hardwired to '0 (lines 1161 and 1172). The RNG wrapper receives no locked-access indication.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms the pattern extends to the RNG peripheral, stripping locked-transfer protection.

evidence.description: aes0_axi_req.aw.lock and aes0_axi_req.ar.lock are hardwired to '0 (lines 1248 and 1259). The AES0 encryption engine never sees a locked bus transaction.
evidence.supports_claim: Critical cryptographic peripheral (AES0) has lock=0 tied off, bypassing AXI locked-access protection.

evidence.description: plic_master.aw_lock is hardwired to '0 (line 746). The PLIC master port cannot assert locked transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: Even the interrupt controller path has the AXI lock field permanently cleared, consistent with a systemic disabling of locked-access semantics.

evidence.description: AES0 wrapper instantiation (line 1194) shows reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl protection controls are present, indicating these peripherals are intended to be security-sensitive and protected.
evidence.supports_claim: The peripherals are treated as security-sensitive (register lock + access control inputs), making the removal of AXI lock protection more impactful.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper accepts jtag_unlock, reglk_ctrl_i, and acct_ctrl_i inputs, showing that a layered permission model (register lock, access control, JTAG unlock) exists for these peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: A permission/lock architecture exists, but the AXI-level lock signal is bypassed at the interconnect layer, creating an inconsistency in the security model.

finding.reasoning_summary: Across all security-critical peripheral AXI request buses (PLIC, RST, RSA, RNG, AES0, and by pattern AES1/AES2/SHA256/HMAC/ACCT/REGLK), the AXI `lock` field on both AW and AR channels is unconditionally tied to zero. In AXI3 this disables locked transfers; in AXI4 it prevents exclusive-access signalling. While these peripherals do have software-level register lock controls (reglk_ctrl) and access control (acct_ctrl), stripping the AXI lock attribute means the interconnect fabric cannot enforce atomic/exclusive access sequences, which is a missing guard for bus-level protection. Any initiator attempting a legitimate locked/exclusive operation to these security peripherals will silently proceed as a non-locked transaction.
finding.security_impact: Removal of AXI locked/exclusive-access signalling across all crypto peripherals (AES, RSA, SHA256, HMAC, RNG) and security infrastructure (REGLK, ACCT) means: (1) atomic read-modify-write sequences relying on AXI exclusive accesses cannot be enforced, potentially creating TOCTOU race conditions on key material or configuration registers; (2) the bus fabric cannot detect or block attempts to use locked transactions as a privilege-escalation vector; (3) the security model is inconsistent — software-level locks exist but bus-level locking is globally suppressed.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The AXI version in use (AXI3 vs AXI4) affects the semantics of the lock field — AXI4 uses it for exclusive access while AXI3 uses it for locked transfers. The actual bus fabric's treatment of lock=0 is not shown. Additionally, snippets for AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, and ACCT AXI tie-offs are not fully shown (only instantiation headers visible), though the pattern is strongly consistent. The reglk_wrapper's internal handling of the lock state relative to rst_ni is not shown in full.

finding.summary: AXI aw_prot field is hardwired to zero for PLIC, RSA, RNG, and AES0 peripherals, permanently disabling privilege-level protection signaling on all AXI transactions to these security-sensitive endpoints.

evidence.description: plic_master.aw_prot is permanently assigned '0, meaning every AXI write to the PLIC carries no privilege, security, or instruction/data type information. The PROT field (AxPROT) in AXI is the bus-level mechanism for communicating whether an access is privileged, secure, or a data/instruction access. Forcing it to zero for a PLIC master port means that downstream interconnect or any firewall relying on AxPROT cannot enforce privilege-based access control for PLIC configuration traffic.
evidence.supports_claim: aw_prot = '0 disables AXI privilege/security signaling for all PLIC write transactions

evidence.description: rst_axi_req.aw.prot and rst_axi_req.ar.prot are both permanently assigned '0. This covers the RST (reset controller) AXI interface, ensuring that all AXI read and write transactions to the reset peripheral carry no privilege or security level indication.
evidence.supports_claim: Both AR and AW prot fields zeroed for reset peripheral AXI interface, bypassing any privilege check at the interconnect

evidence.description: rsa_axi_req.aw.prot and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot are both permanently assigned '0 for the RSA cryptographic peripheral. This is particularly sensitive because the RSA wrapper handles cryptographic key material and operations; denying the interconnect or any firewall the ability to distinguish privileged from unprivileged accesses weakens the access control model.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI prot zeroed for RSA wrapper, removing bus-level privilege signaling for a cryptographic peripheral

evidence.description: rng_axi_req.aw.prot and rng_axi_req.ar.prot are both permanently assigned '0 for the RNG peripheral. Additionally, SNIP-0005 shows that the aes0_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl_i parameters, indicating the design elsewhere relies on access-control signals, yet the bus-level AxPROT is still zeroed for the adjacent RNG.
evidence.supports_claim: RNG AXI prot zeroed; contrast with aes0_wrapper using reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl shows inconsistent protection model

evidence.description: aes0_axi_req.aw.prot and aes0_axi_req.ar.prot are both permanently assigned '0 for the AES0 cryptographic peripheral, despite the AES0 wrapper being instantiated with reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl_i access-control inputs (visible in SNIP-0005 lines 1194-1195). This creates a gap: register-level locking is present but AXI bus-level privilege signaling is absent, so the bus fabric cannot independently enforce privilege separation.
evidence.supports_claim: AES0 AXI prot permanently zeroed despite the AES0 wrapper having explicit register-lock and access-control inputs, showing incomplete protection layering

finding.reasoning_summary: Across all four security-relevant peripheral AXI interfaces (PLIC, RST, RSA, RNG, AES0), the AxPROT field is unconditionally tied to zero. The AXI AxPROT field encodes three bits: privilege level (privileged/unprivileged), security level (secure/non-secure), and access type (data/instruction). Zeroing this field means all transactions appear as unprivileged, non-secure data accesses to any downstream interconnect component or bus firewall that inspects AxPROT. For security-critical peripherals such as RSA and AES0, this removes a layer of bus-level access differentiation. SNIP-0005 additionally shows that AES0 has explicit software-level access-control inputs (reglk_ctrl, acct_ctrl_i), confirming the design intends access control for these peripherals, yet the AXI privilege signal is uniformly suppressed, creating an inconsistent and weakened protection model.
finding.security_impact: Any bus master—regardless of privilege level—that can reach the AXI interconnect appears to the peripheral wrappers and any interposing firewall as making an unprivileged, non-secure access, because AxPROT is always zero. If the interconnect or a peripheral-side firewall uses AxPROT to gate access to RSA key registers, AES cryptographic state, or PLIC interrupt configuration, those checks are ineffective. An unprivileged user-mode attacker able to generate AXI traffic (e.g., via a DMA engine or misconfigured peripheral) could configure interrupt priorities, trigger reset, or access cryptographic peripheral registers without the bus fabric detecting the privilege violation.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets confirm AxPROT is zeroed at the AXI request construction point but do not show whether downstream interconnect components (crossbars, firewalls) or the peripheral wrappers themselves actually inspect AxPROT for access decisions. If no component in the path uses AxPROT for enforcement, the impact is limited to missing defense-in-depth. The acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl inputs to aes0_wrapper suggest some register-level protection exists, but the scope of those controls and whether they fully substitute for AxPROT-based checks is not visible in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: Security peripheral wrappers (AES, SHA256, HMAC, ACCT, REGLK) each accept both a standard rst_ni and a separate per-peripheral reset input (rst_1, rst_2, rst_3, rst_4, rst_6, rst_9, rst_11). The AXI protection field (aw_prot / ar_prot) is hardwired to zero for all peripheral AXI requests, stripping privilege/security/instruction-type information from every transaction routed to these security peripherals.

evidence.description: rst_axi_req.aw.prot and rst_axi_req.ar.prot are permanently tied to '0, meaning all AXI transactions to the RST peripheral carry no privilege or security indication.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI protection bits are zeroed, removing the ability for the peripheral to distinguish privileged from unprivileged or secure from non-secure transactions.

evidence.description: rsa_axi_req.aw.prot and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot are permanently tied to '0 for the RSA peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: Same prot=0 pattern applied to cryptographic RSA peripheral, bypassing AXI security/privilege signaling.

evidence.description: rng_axi_req.aw.prot and rng_axi_req.ar.prot are permanently tied to '0 for the RNG peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI protection field zeroed for RNG, which generates sensitive entropy material.

evidence.description: aes0_axi_req.aw.prot and aes0_axi_req.ar.prot are permanently tied to '0 for AES0.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI protection field zeroed for AES0 cryptographic engine.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper accepts reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i as access-control inputs, but the AXI transactions arriving at it carry prot=0, so the upstream bus fabric cannot enforce privilege-based filtering.
evidence.supports_claim: Per-peripheral access control is present but the upstream AXI prot signal that should carry privilege level is always zero.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper receives jtag_unlock and produces reglk_ctrl_o that gates all other peripherals. Its AXI interface also receives transactions with prot=0, so no bus-level privilege enforcement exists for the master lock controller.
evidence.supports_claim: The register-lock controller itself is reachable without privilege enforcement on the AXI bus.

evidence.description: plic_master.aw_prot is hardwired to '0, so PLIC AXI transactions also carry no privilege or security indication.
evidence.supports_claim: The PLIC AXI master permanently presents prot=0, removing interrupt-controller access privilege information.

evidence.description: plic_req and plic_resp form the register interface into the PLIC. The plic_master driving this interface has aw_prot and ar_prot tied to zero (SNIP-0001), so all PLIC register accesses appear as unprivileged data accesses.
evidence.supports_claim: PLIC register access path carries no privilege signal, allowing unprivileged software to manipulate interrupt priorities/enables without bus-level rejection.

finding.reasoning_summary: Across all security peripherals instantiated in riscv_peripherals (PLIC, RST, RSA, RNG, AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, ACCT, REGLK), the AXI write-address and read-address protection fields (aw_prot / ar_prot) are unconditionally assigned to zero. The AXI AxPROT[2:0] field encodes data/instruction, secure/non-secure, and unprivileged/privileged attributes. Hardwiring these to zero means every transaction is presented to the target peripheral (and any intervening bus fabric firewall) as a non-secure, unprivileged data access, regardless of the actual privilege level of the originating software. Any firewall or peripheral that uses AxPROT to gate access will see all transactions as coming from the least-privileged context. The security peripherals (AES, HMAC, SHA256, REGLK, ACCT) do have their own reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl inputs, but these rely on software-programmed registers rather than hardware-enforced AXI privilege signaling, and the REGLK controller itself is accessible without privilege enforcement.
finding.security_impact: Unprivileged user-mode software can issue AXI transactions to cryptographic peripherals (AES, HMAC, SHA256, RSA, RNG) and the interrupt controller (PLIC) that are indistinguishable from privileged supervisor/machine-mode accesses at the bus fabric level. Any firewall relying on AxPROT for privilege-based access control is bypassed. An attacker could read or overwrite AES keys, HMAC secrets, PLIC interrupt priorities, or the REGLK register-lock state without triggering privilege violations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets do not show the bus fabric or crossbar firewall configuration; it is possible that no AxPROT-based firewall exists and the peripheral-level reglk/acct controls are the sole enforcement mechanism. However, hardwiring prot=0 is still incorrect because it prevents any future or existing bus-level privilege enforcement from functioning. The internal behavior of each wrapper when prot=0 is not shown, but the structural violation is evident in the source.

finding.summary: The AES1 wrapper (aes1_wrapper) is instantiated without the 'we_flag' override that AES0 uses, and the reglk_ctrl_i signal for AES1 is passed without the OR of a we_flag bypass, while AES0's reglk_ctrl_i is explicitly OR'd with we_flag_1. More critically, the PLIC interface in riscv_peripherals does not apply any reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl access control checks — the PLIC AXI master interface is connected and driven through a simplified AXI state machine with no privilege, lock, or access-control guard, unlike all other security-sensitive peripherals (AES, SHA256, HMAC, ACCT, REGLK) that receive reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs.

evidence.description: The PLIC AXI state machine processes AXI read/write requests from plic_master and drives plic_req directly. There is no reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, or privilege check applied anywhere in this state machine before issuing PLIC register accesses.
evidence.supports_claim: PLIC register access is ungated — no access control or register lock is enforced on the PLIC interface, unlike all other peripherals in the same module.

evidence.description: AES0 wrapper instantiation includes reglk_ctrl_i ( reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1 ) and acct_ctrl_i ( acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][1] ), enforcing both register lock and privilege-level access control.
evidence.supports_claim: Other peripherals have access control guards applied at instantiation, confirming the design intent. The PLIC lacks equivalent protection.

evidence.description: AES1 wrapper instantiation includes reglk_ctrl_i ( reglk_ctrl[2*8+7:2*8] ) without OR-ing a we_flag, and acct_ctrl_i ( acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][2] ). AES1 is missing the we_flag bypass that AES0 has.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent access control: AES1 lacks the we_flag override present in AES0, potentially causing AES1 registers to be inaccessible during legitimate write-enable scenarios.

evidence.description: AES2 wrapper instantiation passes reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i, showing the standard guard pattern is applied.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms access control guard is expected for cryptographic peripherals, highlighting its absence on the PLIC.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper exports reglk_ctrl_o which feeds reglk_ctrl_i into all guarded peripherals. The PLIC interface does not receive this signal.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock mechanism managed by reglk_wrapper is bypassed entirely for the PLIC peripheral.

evidence.description: Lines 801-816 show plic_req.valid is set to 1 and plic_req.write is driven from AXI master signals with no intervening privilege or lock check. Any AXI master with access to the PLIC address range can read or write PLIC registers without restriction.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard: PLIC register access is not gated by reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl, allowing unprivileged access to interrupt controller configuration registers.

finding.reasoning_summary: All security-sensitive peripherals in riscv_peripherals (AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, ACCT, REGLK) receive reglk_ctrl_i (register lock) and acct_ctrl_i (access control based on privilege level) inputs at instantiation. The PLIC interface, by contrast, is implemented as a bare AXI-to-register-interface bridge with no access control guards. Any AXI master that can address the PLIC can freely read and write PLIC registers (interrupt enables, priorities, threshold, claim/complete). Additionally, AES1 is missing the we_flag OR that AES0 uses, creating an inconsistency in the register-lock bypass mechanism.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged software agent or bus master can reconfigure the PLIC (e.g., disable interrupts, change priorities, claim/complete interrupts) without authorization, potentially causing denial of service or privilege escalation via interrupt manipulation. The missing we_flag on AES1 may also cause AES1 registers to remain locked when they should be writable, or conversely expose them when they should be protected.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full PLIC instantiation context is not shown in the snippets, so it is possible that access control is applied at a higher hierarchy level or within the PLIC IP itself. The we_flag_1 and we_flag definitions and their security implications are not fully visible. The exact threat model for the PLIC address space is not confirmed from the available snippets alone.

finding.summary: In aes0_wrapper, key2 is not zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, unlike key0 and key1. This means the third key material (key2) is always exposed regardless of debug mode, creating an inconsistent and incomplete debug-mode protection.

evidence.description: Lines 54-56 show that key_big0 and key_big1 are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted (`debug_mode_i ? 192'b0 : ...`), but key_big2 (line 56) has NO debug_mode_i guard: `assign key_big2 = {key2[0], key2[1], key2[2], key2[3], key2[4], key2[5]};`. key2 is always exposed to the downstream logic regardless of debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Incomplete debug-mode protection: key2 leaks even when debug_mode_i=1, violating the intended key zeroing policy applied to key0 and key1.

evidence.description: In aes1_wrapper (lines 149+), core_key0 is zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted. The pattern is consistent for key0 but needs verification for all keys. The debug_mode_i input port exists (line 58 of SNIP-0003) indicating an intentional debug-mode key suppression mechanism.
evidence.supports_claim: The design intent is to zero keys in debug mode; the missing guard on key_big2 in aes0_wrapper is an inconsistency/omission.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i is declared as an input port in aes1_wrapper (line 58) and aes0_wrapper (line 22 in SNIP-0002), confirming that debug mode is a recognized security boundary intended to suppress sensitive key material.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i signal is the security control meant to zero key material; its absence from key2 assignment is the vulnerability.

evidence.description: Key registers (key_reg0, key_reg1, key_reg2) in aes1_wrapper are protected by reglk_ctrl_i bits on write. The key material is treated as sensitive, requiring lock-based access control.
evidence.supports_claim: Key material is explicitly identified as a protected resource requiring access control, making the incomplete debug zeroing a meaningful security gap.

evidence.description: Read-side guards in aes1_wrapper use reglk_ctrl_i bits to zero readback of results and status. Combined with the write-side key_we* guards, the reglk_ctrl/debug_mode_i scheme is the intended security perimeter.
evidence.supports_claim: The security architecture relies on consistent application of guards; missing the debug_mode guard on key2 breaks this perimeter.

finding.reasoning_summary: In aes0_wrapper, lines 54-56 show an intentional design pattern of zeroing key material (key_big0, key_big1) when debug_mode_i is asserted. However, key_big2 on line 56 is assigned directly from key2[] without any debug_mode_i conditional zeroing. This is a clear asymmetry: key0 and key1 are suppressed in debug mode, but key2 is always passed through to downstream AES logic. This means that when the processor is in debug mode — a state that should restrict access to sensitive cryptographic material — the third key slot (key2) remains readable/usable, potentially exposing secret key material to a debugger or debug-mode attacker.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access can recover key2 cryptographic key material from the aes0 peripheral even when debug_mode_i is asserted. This bypasses the intended key zeroing protection and could lead to full AES key recovery if key2 contains secret key data, undermining the confidentiality of encrypted data protected by the AES peripheral.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown in the provided snippets exactly how key2 is used relative to key0 and key1 (e.g., whether key2 is a backup/alternative key or always active). If key2 is intentionally unprotected (e.g., it holds only a public/non-secret key), the severity would be lower. The full register-map decode and key selection logic (key_sel usage) is not fully visible in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: Boot ROM selection mux is broken: both branches of the ternary assign the same value (rom_rdata_linux), so ariane_boot_sel_i has no effect and the baremetal ROM path is never selected.

evidence.description: Line 534: `assign rom_rdata = (ariane_boot_sel_i) ? rom_rdata_linux : rom_rdata_linux;` — both the true and false branches of the mux are identical (rom_rdata_linux). The comment states baremetal mode should be selected when ariane_boot_sel_i is deasserted, but the baremetal ROM data (rom_rdata_bm) is never used. The boot-mode control signal is rendered completely ineffective.
evidence.supports_claim: The ternary expression always outputs rom_rdata_linux regardless of ariane_boot_sel_i, meaning the hardware boot-mode selector has no effect on which boot ROM image is presented to the CPU.

evidence.description: Three ROM data signals are declared: rom_rdata, rom_rdata_bm, and rom_rdata_linux. The existence of rom_rdata_bm indicates a separate baremetal ROM path was intended, but it is never driven to rom_rdata.
evidence.supports_claim: The baremetal ROM content (rom_rdata_bm) is a distinct protected boot image that should only be active when ariane_boot_sel_i selects baremetal mode, yet it is bypassed entirely.

finding.reasoning_summary: The assign statement on line 534 uses ariane_boot_sel_i as the select signal of a ternary mux, but mistakenly places rom_rdata_linux on both sides instead of rom_rdata_bm on one side. This makes the boot-mode pin meaningless: the CPU always boots from the Linux ROM regardless of the hardware strap. A separate rom_rdata_bm signal is declared (visible in SNIP-0001) but never connected to rom_rdata, confirming the intended dual-path design was not implemented correctly.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or misconfigured board that relies on ariane_boot_sel_i to force baremetal/secure boot mode will always receive the Linux bootrom instead. This defeats the hardware-enforced boot-mode selection mechanism, potentially allowing execution of an untrusted or unexpected firmware image when a secure/baremetal image was intended, and preventing any ROM-based boot policy enforcement tied to the strap pin.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The definition and content of rom_rdata_bm (the baremetal ROM instance) are not shown in the provided snippets, but its declaration in SNIP-0001 and its absence from the mux strongly corroborate the finding. No additional ambiguity exists for the core bug.

finding.summary: A commented-out ROM access control guard reveals that the active assignment for rom_req previously lacked privilege-level gating. The comment directly above the active line shows the unguarded version was once in use, indicating the guard may be fragile or recently added. More critically, the acc_ctrl values controlling peripheral access (AES, HMAC, SHA256, ACCT, REGLK) are sourced from the acct_wrapper's memory output (acc_ctrl_o), which itself is initialized and written only when the acct module is enabled via acct_ctrl_i — but acct_ctrl_i is derived from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][acct_index], creating a circular dependency: the access control table that governs all peripherals is itself protected by the access control table. On reset or before the acct_wrapper has been configured, acc_ctrl values default to zero, and when acc_ctrl is zero, acc_ctrl_c values are zero, which means acct_ctrl_i is zero, locking out the acct_wrapper and preventing any legitimate update to the access control table — but simultaneously, the we_flag override in acc_ctrl_o forces the lowest bits of the access control output high ({8{we_flag}}), potentially bypassing the intended zero-default lockout for peripheral index 0. Additionally, line 516 shows a previously active unguarded rom_req assignment was simply commented out, leaving evidence of a missing guard that was in production.

evidence.description: Line 516 shows the commented-out unguarded assignment `assign rom_req = rom_req_acct;` immediately above the active guarded one. This demonstrates the guard was absent previously and was added as a patch, indicating the gate was missing in an earlier state and may still be fragile.
evidence.supports_claim: The ROM access guard was previously bypassed (no privilege check), and the commented line confirms this was once the active assignment.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl, which comes from acct_wrapper's acc_ctrl_o. All peripheral acct_ctrl_i inputs are slices of acc_ctrl_c indexed by priv_lvl_i. This forms the permission state for all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control matrix that gates all peripherals is sourced from the acct_wrapper output.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is assigned as `{acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}`. The `|{8{we_flag}}` expression forces the lowest 8 bits of the access control output high whenever we_flag is asserted, regardless of the stored access control values, bypassing the intended zero-default lockout for peripheral 0.
evidence.supports_claim: The we_flag can unconditionally override the access control values for peripheral 0, bypassing the normal permission mechanism.

evidence.description: `assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i;` — the acct_wrapper itself is gated by acct_ctrl_i, which is derived from the acc_ctrl table it manages. On reset, if acc_ctrl is zero, acct_ctrl_i is zero, locking the acct_wrapper from being configured.
evidence.supports_claim: Circular dependency: the acct_wrapper that manages all access control is itself gated by the access control table it controls, creating a potential deadlock or initialization vulnerability.

evidence.description: `assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i;` for reglk_wrapper. The register lock controller is also gated by acct_ctrl_i from acc_ctrl_c, meaning if the access control table is zeroed, the register lock peripheral itself cannot be written.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_wrapper (which controls all peripheral register locks) is access-controlled by the same acct table, compounding the circular dependency.

evidence.description: The acct_wrapper instance in riscv_peripherals receives acct_ctrl_i from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][acct_index], which is derived from its own output acc_ctrl_o. This is the root of the circular dependency.
evidence.supports_claim: The acct_wrapper's enable is derived from its own output, creating a self-referential access control chain.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper outputs reglk_ctrl_o which is used to lock all other peripheral registers. If reglk_wrapper is inaccessible due to the circular acct dependency, register locks cannot be configured.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock controller is a critical security resource that may be unreachable during initialization due to the circular permission dependency.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain reveals two related vulnerabilities: (1) A previously unguarded ROM request path (evidenced by commented-out code on line 516) that lacked privilege-level checking. (2) A circular dependency in the access control architecture where the acct_wrapper, which manages all peripheral access control values, is itself gated by the access control values it produces. On reset, all acct_mem values are zero, making all acc_ctrl_c values zero, which prevents the acct_wrapper from being enabled via its own acct_ctrl_i input. The we_flag override in acc_ctrl_o partially mitigates this for peripheral 0 but introduces its own bypass. The reglk_wrapper similarly cannot be configured until acct grants access, and acct cannot be written until it grants itself access.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or misconfigured boot sequence could exploit the circular dependency to leave all peripherals (AES0/1/2, HMAC, SHA256, ACCT, REGLK, ROM) in their reset state with potentially incorrect access controls. The we_flag bypass in the acct_wrapper could allow unauthorized writes to the access control table if we_flag is asserted inappropriately. The previously-unguarded ROM path (confirmed by commented code) means the guard may have been absent in deployed hardware.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact initialization sequence and whether a privileged boot agent correctly pre-populates acct_mem before normal operation is not visible in these snippets. The source of we_flag and its trust boundary is not shown. The full acct_wrapper reset behavior and whether acc_ctrl defaults to a safe non-zero value after reset is not confirmed from the available snippets alone.

finding.summary: RSA AXI request channel protection field (prot) is hardwired to zero, disabling AXI protection signaling for the RSA peripheral

evidence.description: Lines 1077 and 1088 unconditionally assign rsa_axi_req.aw.prot and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot to '0 (all zeros). The AXI4 ARPROT/AWPROT field encodes privilege level, security domain (Secure/Non-Secure), and instruction/data type. Setting it to zero forces the RSA accelerator transactions to appear as Unprivileged, Secure, Data accesses with no ability to enforce or signal the actual privilege or security context of the initiating transaction.
evidence.supports_claim: The PROT field on both the write address channel (aw.prot='0) and read address channel (ar.prot='0) are hardwired to zero, meaning any downstream bus interconnect or security monitor that uses PROT for access control or auditing will see the RSA peripheral as always operating in Secure/Unprivileged mode regardless of the actual initiator context, potentially bypassing downstream security filtering.

evidence.description: The rsa_axi_req structure represents AXI bus transactions to/from the RSA cryptographic accelerator. All AXI address and control fields including lock (aw.lock=0, ar.lock=0), cache, and prot are statically tied off as part of converting from AXI-lite to full AXI.
evidence.supports_claim: Transactions to the RSA accelerator are issued on the AXI bus with all protection metadata permanently zeroed, making it impossible for downstream components to distinguish privilege levels or enforce security policies on RSA memory-mapped register accesses.

finding.reasoning_summary: The code at lines 1070-1090 explicitly ties off AXI sideband fields for the RSA peripheral's AXI request channel. The comment labels these as 'non-used by AXI-lite' signals. However, the aw.prot and ar.prot fields (lines 1077, 1088) are security-relevant AXI4 signals: bit[1] indicates Non-Secure (1) vs Secure (0) access, and bit[0] indicates Privileged (0) vs Unprivileged (1) access. By hardwiring both to zero, all RSA AXI transactions present as Secure+Unprivileged+Data to any downstream access control enforcement point (e.g., TrustZone memory protection units, bus firewalls), which is incorrect and misleading — legitimate privileged OS transactions to configure the RSA engine would appear identical to unprivileged accesses, and security-enforcing interconnect components cannot filter malicious unprivileged access attempts that would appear identical to legitimate ones.
finding.security_impact: Any downstream AXI interconnect component that uses ARPROT/AWPROT to enforce access control (e.g., a TrustZone-aware memory protection unit or bus firewall) will be unable to distinguish between privileged and unprivileged initiators when they target the RSA accelerator. This could allow unprivileged software to issue AXI transactions that appear with the same protection encoding as privileged/secure transactions, bypassing downstream security filtering. Additionally, audit logs of AXI transactions would incorrectly record the security context of all RSA operations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual security impact depends on whether downstream components in this SoC use PROT for access control enforcement. The chain context does not include evidence of downstream bus firewall or memory protection unit configurations. The design intent comment says these are 'non-axi-lite signals' being tied off, suggesting the AXI-lite master does not drive PROT, which may be an inherent limitation of the AXI-lite protocol bridge rather than a deliberate bypass. However, the absence of any privilege/security propagation to the PROT field for a cryptographic peripheral remains a security concern.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper resets its access-control memory (acct_mem) to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF) on reset, meaning all peripheral access bits are set to fully-permissive after reset. The acc_ctrl_o output computed from acct_mem is immediately used as the acc_ctrl_c permission vector fed to every security-critical peripheral (AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, REGLK). An all-ones reset value grants full access to all peripherals from the start, before any authenticated software has a chance to configure restrictions.

evidence.description: On reset (rst_ni or rst_6 asserted), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for every entry. This makes every access-control bit '1', which in the permission model means all accesses are permitted.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset sets all permission bits to all-ones (fully permissive), allowing unrestricted access to all protected peripherals immediately after reset before any secure configuration.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is directly derived from acct_mem entries: assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}. After reset, all acct_mem entries are 0xFFFFFFFF, so acc_ctrl_o is fully-permissive.
evidence.supports_claim: The permission output signal is sourced from the all-ones reset state, propagating full-access permissions to all downstream peripherals.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is computed from acc_ctrl (which receives acc_ctrl_o from acct_wrapper) via: assign acc_ctrl_c[i][j] = acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i]). The OR with additional bits means that if acc_ctrl bits are all-ones, acc_ctrl_c bits are all-ones regardless.
evidence.supports_claim: The permission transformation preserves the all-ones permissive state from reset, so the guard signal presented to each peripheral is also fully permissive.

evidence.description: AES0 wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][1], which after reset is 1 (all access permitted) regardless of privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: AES0 access control is fully open immediately after reset.

evidence.description: SHA256 wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][3], which after reset is 1 (all access permitted).
evidence.supports_claim: SHA256 access control is fully open immediately after reset.

evidence.description: REGLK wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i from acc_ctrl_c, and reglk_wrapper controls register-lock bits for all other peripherals. If REGLK is accessible with all-ones permissions immediately after reset, an attacker can overwrite lock configurations before secure boot completes.
evidence.supports_claim: The register-lock controller itself is unprotected after reset, enabling an attacker to reconfigure locks on all peripherals.

evidence.description: acct_mem[j] <= 32'hffffffff on reset means all access control memory initializes to all-permitted. This is the concrete source of the insecure reset state: instead of a deny-by-default (0x00000000), a permit-by-default (0xFFFFFFFF) value is used.
evidence.supports_claim: Insecure default permission state after reset: all access control bits are '1' (permit all) rather than '0' (deny all), violating least-privilege principle.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module manages access control memory (acct_mem) that determines which privilege levels can access each peripheral. On reset, all acct_mem entries are initialized to 32'hffffffff (all bits set to 1). The acc_ctrl_o output is directly derived from these all-ones values and fed back through riscv_peripherals as acc_ctrl, which is then reorganized into acc_ctrl_c and distributed to every security-critical peripheral (AES0, AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC, REGLK) as their acct_ctrl_i access-control signal. Since acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][x] will be 1 for all privilege levels and all peripherals immediately after reset, there is a window (from reset until secure software has written restrictive values into acct_mem) during which any code running at any privilege level has full access to all cryptographic peripherals and the register-lock controller itself.
finding.security_impact: Any software running after reset but before secure configuration of acct_mem has unrestricted access to AES keys, HMAC keys, SHA256 inputs/outputs, and the register-lock (reglk) configuration. An attacker with code execution early in boot could read cryptographic keys, inject data, or configure register locks to prevent future access restrictions, permanently compromising the security of the platform.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact semantics of the individual acct_mem bits (which bit value means 'allow' vs 'deny') must be inferred from context; however, the all-ones initialization combined with the direct use of these bits as permission enables (en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i) strongly indicates all-ones means all-permitted. The we_flag OR in acc_ctrl_o computation is also not fully characterized but does not mitigate the concern.

finding.summary: The sha256_wrapper exposes SHA-256 hash output (hash[255:0]) via the rdata read path, and hash readback is only gated by reglk_ctrl_i[4]. However, the reglk_ctrl value connected to sha256_wrapper at instantiation is reglk_ctrl[3*8+7:3*8] with NO additional 'we_flag' OR-masking, unlike the aes0_wrapper which ORs in a we_flag_1 sticky lock. This means the hash readback protection depends entirely on software-controlled reglk_ctrl bits that are never permanently locked by hardware, allowing any software with register-write access to clear reglk_ctrl[3*8+4] and read back sensitive SHA-256 hash output.

evidence.description: sha256_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i connected to reglk_ctrl[3*8+7:3*8] only, with no we_flag OR-masking. Compare with aes0_wrapper at line 1194 which uses '| we_flag_1' to permanently lock after write.
evidence.supports_claim: The sha256_wrapper's register lock has no sticky/hardware-enforced lock bit, unlike other peripherals in the same file.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper instantiation at line 1194 shows: reglk_ctrl_i ( reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1 ), providing a hardware-enforced sticky lock. sha256_wrapper lacks this pattern.
evidence.supports_claim: Demonstrates the intended pattern includes a sticky we_flag that sha256_wrapper is missing.

evidence.description: Lines 207-221: hash[255:0] is returned via rdata when address maps to registers 18-25, gated only by reglk_ctrl_i[4]. If reglk_ctrl_i[4] is 0, the full 256-bit hash is readable.
evidence.supports_claim: SHA-256 hash output is a sensitive value readable from the register interface when the lock bit is not set.

evidence.description: Line 168: rdata read path only checks 'if (en)' before allowing reads, and individual fields are conditionally zeroed by reglk_ctrl_i bits. There is no additional privilege or hardware lock enforcement beyond the software-writable reglk_ctrl field.
evidence.supports_claim: Software can clear reglk_ctrl[3*8+4] to enable hash readback at any time, as there is no hardware-enforced lock comparable to the we_flag seen on aes0.

evidence.description: Lines 126-156 show the data[] input message registers are also only protected by reglk_ctrl_i[3], meaning message input can also be written/read without hardware sticky lock protection.
evidence.supports_claim: Both the SHA-256 input message and output hash are accessible without sticky hardware lock, enabling sensitive data exfiltration.

finding.reasoning_summary: The sha256_wrapper receives its reglk_ctrl_i lock bits from reglk_ctrl[3*8+7:3*8], a software-writable register with no hardware-enforced sticky locking (no we_flag OR mask). The aes0_wrapper in the same file explicitly ORs in a we_flag_1 signal to create a write-once hardware lock. sha256_wrapper lacks this mechanism. As a result, any software that can write the reglk_ctrl register can set bit [4] to 0 and read back the full 256-bit SHA-256 hash output via the rdata interface. The read path at lines 207-221 only checks reglk_ctrl_i[4] before returning hash data, providing no defense-in-depth.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with software access to the reglk_ctrl register can clear the hash readback lock bit (bit 4 of the sha256 peripheral's reglk_ctrl slice) at any time and exfiltrate the 256-bit SHA-256 hash output. Similarly, the message input registers (data[0]-data[15]) can be read back by clearing bit 2. This undermines any security property relying on the SHA-256 peripheral's register locking mechanism.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact definition and initialization of reglk_ctrl and we_flag signals are not fully shown; it is possible reglk_ctrl is itself protected elsewhere. However, the structural asymmetry between aes0_wrapper (with we_flag OR) and sha256_wrapper (without) is directly visible in the source snippets and constitutes concrete evidence of a missing guard.

finding.summary: The sha256_wrapper's `data` registers (message input words) are protected by `reglk_ctrl_i` on write, but the read path in sha256_wrapper does not appear to be guarded by `reglk_ctrl_i` or `acct_ctrl_i`, allowing sensitive SHA-256 input data and hash output to be read back without a register-lock check. Additionally, AES0 wrapper receives a `reglk_ctrl_i` that is ORed with a `we_flag_1` signal, meaning the lock bits can be forced asserted via `we_flag_1`, but the key and state registers cannot be cleared via normal write paths once locked, and the lock register itself in reglk_wrapper is fed back its own output as input, creating a potential issue where lock state is not correctly initialized or cleared on reset.

evidence.description: sha256_wrapper declares `data[0:15]` as the 512-bit message input, `hash` as the 256-bit digest output, and `rdata` as the read-data bus. These are sensitive resources that should be protected from unauthorized readback.
evidence.supports_claim: Identifies the sensitive data registers (message input and hash output) that need read protection in sha256_wrapper.

evidence.description: sha256_wrapper is instantiated with `reglk_ctrl_i ( reglk_ctrl[3*8+7:3*8])` and `acct_ctrl_i ( acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][3])`. Both lock and access-control signals are passed in, but the snippet shows no explicit gating of the read path at the instantiation level.
evidence.supports_claim: Shows that reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl are connected to sha256_wrapper, but does not show read-path protection enforced externally.

evidence.description: AES0 wrapper receives `reglk_ctrl_i ( reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1 )`. The OR with `we_flag_1` means the register lock bits for AES0 can be forced high by an external flag without going through the normal lock mechanism, potentially preventing writes but also blocking legitimate updates. However, other peripherals (AES1, AES2, SHA256, HMAC) receive `reglk_ctrl` directly without any OR mask, showing inconsistent protection logic.
evidence.supports_claim: Demonstrates inconsistency: AES0 has an extra we_flag_1 OR applied to its lock signal, while sha256_wrapper and others do not, indicating potential asymmetric protection.

evidence.description: AES0 write-side checks `reglk_ctrl_i[3]`, `reglk_ctrl_i[5]`, `reglk_ctrl_i[7]`, `reglk_ctrl_i[1]` for key, state, and control registers. The pattern shows write protection is present, but the read-side (always @(*)) starting at line 163 is not shown, leaving open whether read protection is enforced.
evidence.supports_claim: Write guards exist for AES0 register writes, but read-side protection is not evidenced in the provided snippets, a pattern also applicable to sha256_wrapper.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper outputs `reglk_ctrl_o` which feeds `riscv_peripherals.reglk_ctrl`, and also receives `reglk_ctrl_i` which is connected back from `riscv_peripherals.reglk_ctrl`. This creates a feedback loop where the lock controller reads its own output as input, risking incorrect lock-state initialization or unintended self-modification.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk feedback loop (reglk_ctrl_o -> reglk_ctrl -> reglk_ctrl_i for reglk_wrapper itself) means the lock register module can read/modify its own lock state, which is a self-referential protection state vulnerability.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper has both `reglk_ctrl_i [7:0]` (input) and `reglk_ctrl_o [8*NB_PERIPHERALS-1:0]` (output). The module controls locks for all peripherals including itself (index bits fed back), enabling a scenario where software could modify the reglk_wrapper's own lock bits before they are set, bypassing the lock-once semantics.
evidence.supports_claim: Self-referential lock control in reglk_wrapper allows potential bypass of the lock mechanism protecting the lock registers themselves.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper write-side uses `reglk_ctrl[5]`, `reglk_ctrl[13]`, `reglk_ctrl[1]`, `reglk_ctrl[7]` bit indices from `reglk_ctrl_i` which is an 8-bit input port (`reglk_ctrl_i[7:0]`). Accessing `reglk_ctrl[13]` from an 8-bit signal is out-of-bounds (bits 8-13 do not exist in an 8-bit bus), meaning those lock checks will always read 0 (unlocked), allowing writes to `acct_mem[03]`, `acct_mem[04]`, `acct_mem[05]` regardless of the intended lock state.
evidence.supports_claim: Out-of-bounds bit indexing on reglk_ctrl_i[13] from an 8-bit port means the lock check for acct_mem[03..05] is always false, creating a missing guard vulnerability on access-control memory entries.

finding.reasoning_summary: The primary concrete vulnerability found in the source snippets is in acct_wrapper: the write guard for `acct_mem[03]`, `acct_mem[04]`, and `acct_mem[05]` uses `reglk_ctrl[13]` but `reglk_ctrl_i` is only 8 bits wide (indices 0-7). Accessing bit 13 of a narrower bus in SystemVerilog returns 0 (unlocked), so these access-control memory entries can always be overwritten regardless of the lock setting. This is a concrete missing-guard violation. Additionally, the reglk_wrapper self-feedback loop and the `we_flag_1` OR on AES0's lock signal represent structural permission-control weaknesses evidenced in the source.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with register-write access can overwrite `acct_mem[03..05]` in acct_wrapper regardless of register lock state due to out-of-bounds bit indexing, potentially altering access-control policy for peripherals. The reglk self-feedback allows the lock-register module's own protection to be modified before it is set, enabling lock bypass. The we_flag_1 override on AES0's reglk creates an asymmetric protection path not present on other peripherals.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The read-side logic of sha256_wrapper and aes0_wrapper is not included in the provided snippets (cut off at line 163 for aes0, not shown for sha256), so it cannot be confirmed whether read protection (reglk or acct gating on rdata) is absent. The we_flag_1 source and semantics are not shown. The full reglk_wrapper write logic is not provided to confirm whether self-modification of lock bits is possible.

finding.summary: The sha256_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i permission inputs, but the newMessage signal (the seed target) is driven without evidence of any reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i guard in the write path, meaning the register-lock protection may be missing or incomplete for the newMessage control register.

evidence.description: sha256_wrapper declares newMessage as an internal logic signal derived from register writes, alongside reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs that are supposed to gate register access. The newMessage signal is the seed target of this chain.
evidence.supports_claim: newMessage is a writable control signal inside sha256_wrapper that should be protected by reglk_ctrl_i.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates sha256_wrapper and connects reglk_ctrl[3*8+7:3*8] to reglk_ctrl_i and acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][3] to acct_ctrl_i. No additional we_flag ORed into the reglk_ctrl slice for SHA256, unlike the aes0 instance which has '| we_flag_1'.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl slice connected to sha256 may be correct, but there is no we_flag override unlike aes0, which is an inconsistency, but more critically the internal guard must be verified.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper shows the expected pattern: every register write is gated by reglk_ctrl_i[bit] ? keep_old : accept_new. This is the reference guard pattern used across the design.
evidence.supports_claim: Other wrappers guard all register writes with reglk_ctrl_i bits; absence of equivalent guard for newMessage in sha256_wrapper would be a violation.

evidence.description: The sha256_wrapper source snippet (lines 12-56) shows the signal declarations including newMessage, reglk_ctrl_i, and acct_ctrl_i, but provides no write-side always block guarding newMessage with reglk_ctrl_i. The snippet covers all signal declarations and does not include any conditional reglk_ctrl_i check protecting the newMessage register write, unlike the aes0_wrapper pattern shown in SNIP-0015.
evidence.supports_claim: No reglk_ctrl_i guard is visible for the newMessage write path in sha256_wrapper, indicating a missing lock check that exists in peer modules.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper guards its memory writes with reglk_ctrl[bit] checks in every case branch. This further confirms the expected design pattern where all sensitive register writes must be gated.
evidence.supports_claim: The design pattern consistently requires reglk_ctrl gating; its absence for newMessage in sha256_wrapper is a concrete gap.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain centers on sha256_wrapper.newMessage. The sha256_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs intended to lock register writes. Peer modules (aes0_wrapper, acct_wrapper) consistently gate every register write with reglk_ctrl_i bit checks. The available sha256_wrapper source (SNIP-0020) covers all signal declarations including newMessage but contains no write-side always block showing a reglk_ctrl_i guard on newMessage writes, meaning the lock protection for newMessage is absent or not present in the provided evidence. This constitutes a missing guard vulnerability where the register-lock mechanism does not protect the newMessage control input.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or unauthorized software agent could write to the newMessage register of the SHA-256 peripheral even when the register-lock controller has asserted the lock for that peripheral. This allows an attacker to initiate new hash computations or corrupt in-progress hash operations, potentially enabling denial-of-service against the hash engine or manipulation of hash outputs used for integrity verification.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full write-side always block for sha256_wrapper is not included in the provided snippets; it is possible that a guard exists in lines not shown. The snippet cuts off at line 56 and the write logic likely resides later in the file. However, based on available evidence, no guard is shown for newMessage, which is sufficient to flag a potential finding at medium confidence.

finding.summary: In sha256_wrapper, the registers newMessage_r and startHash_r are declared as plain 'reg' without an explicit reset in the always block that handles rst_ni and rst_3. The write-side logic for sha256_wrapper does not show these registers being reset to a known safe value on reset, leaving them potentially retaining stale values after reset. Additionally, the reglk_ctrl protection is only applied through reglk_ctrl_i from riscv_peripherals but the sha256_wrapper does not guard newMessage_r/startHash_r writes with reglk_ctrl_i checks, allowing hash trigger signals to be set regardless of lock state.

evidence.description: sha256_wrapper declares newMessage_r and startHash_r as plain 'reg' (line 29). The module receives reglk_ctrl_i (line 19) and rst_3 (line 24), but the snippet shows no reset or lock guard applied to newMessage_r/startHash_r in the internal signal declarations.
evidence.supports_claim: newMessage_r and startHash_r are not shown to be reset or protected by reglk_ctrl_i, making them potentially accessible without proper lock enforcement.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates sha256_wrapper passing reglk_ctrl[3*8+7:3*8] as reglk_ctrl_i (line 1450), rst_ni (line 1449), and rst_3 (line 1453 implied by edge:connects:31). The lock control is wired, but effectiveness depends on sha256_wrapper applying it to all sensitive registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The lock signal is connected at the top level, but its application inside sha256_wrapper to newMessage_r/startHash_r is absent in the available source.

evidence.description: aes0_wrapper shows the correct pattern: every writable register (start, p_c, key, state) is guarded by reglk_ctrl_i[bit] checks in the write always block (lines 99-153), and all are reset on ~(rst_ni && ~rst_1).
evidence.supports_claim: Reference pattern shows that register lock guards should be applied per-register in write always blocks; sha256_wrapper's newMessage_r/startHash_r lack this same treatment.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper similarly guards every acct_mem write with reglk_ctrl[bit] checks (lines 90-108) and resets memory on ~(rst_ni && ~rst_6) (lines 81-85).
evidence.supports_claim: Consistent pattern across other wrappers shows lock-guarded writes; sha256_wrapper newMessage_r/startHash_r are missing this protection.

evidence.description: newMessage_r and startHash_r (line 29) are plain 'reg' signals that control hash operations (newMessage triggers new message input, startHash starts hashing). The source snippet does not show reglk_ctrl_i gating on writes to these registers, unlike all other wrappers in the chain.
evidence.supports_claim: An attacker who can write to sha256_wrapper registers could set newMessage_r or startHash_r without the register lock preventing it, potentially triggering unauthorized hash operations or corrupting in-progress hash state.

finding.reasoning_summary: All other peripheral wrappers in this security chain (aes0_wrapper, aes1_wrapper, acct_wrapper, hmac_wrapper) consistently guard every writable sensitive register with reglk_ctrl_i bit checks and properly reset them. The sha256_wrapper declares newMessage_r and startHash_r as plain 'reg' without evidence in the source snippets of reglk_ctrl_i guards on their write paths. This is a missing guard vulnerability: the register lock mechanism intended to protect the SHA-256 peripheral's hash trigger signals is not applied, allowing writes to these control registers even when the peripheral should be locked.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or untrusted software context could write to the SHA-256 wrapper to assert newMessage or startHash without the register lock blocking the write, enabling unauthorized hash computation initiation, potential hash state corruption mid-operation, or side-channel leakage of sensitive data being hashed by a higher-privilege context.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippet for sha256_wrapper (SNIP-0020) shows only the signal declarations and does not include the write always block that would update newMessage_r and startHash_r. It is possible that the write always block (not included in the snippets) does apply reglk_ctrl_i guards. However, the absence of this block in the provided evidence, combined with the fact that newMessage_r/startHash_r are declared as plain 'reg' without initialization, and the fact that the graph seed target is on newMessage_r at line 159 which is not shown, leaves the vulnerability plausible but not fully confirmed from source alone.
